• Dix-Hallpike maneuver: The patient is initially seated when the maneuver is performed. Their head is tilted 45 degrees in the direction of the ear being examined.
• The patient is then rapidly placed in a supine position with their head 30 degrees below the horizontal by the examiner.
• Throughout the process, the head rotation is held at 45 degrees. The examiner is now looking for nystagmus in the patient.

Other Options:
• Option A: Brandt-Daroff Exercise - The Brandt-Daroff exercise is a set of movements that are often recommended as a home treatment for benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV). BPPV is a condition where small calcium crystals in the inner ear become dislodged and cause dizziness. The exercises involve a series of head and body movements designed to help reposition these crystals, relieving symptoms of dizziness and vertigo.
• Option B: The Epley maneuver, which pushes the otolith out of the posterior canal, is the recommended treatment method. With positional modifications easing the symptoms, this puts an end to the feeling of vertigo.
• Option D: The Heimlich maneuver is an emergency first aid procedure used to help a choking person who is unable to breathe due to an obstruction in their airway. It involves delivering abdominal thrusts to forcefully expel the foreign object blocking the airway. The Heimlich maneuver can be life-saving in situations where choking occurs.

stem: An anatomy demonstration is being conducted in which the anatomy of the nose and paranasal sinuses is being discussed. During the demonstration, the instructor points out an area on the diagram of the lateral wall of the nose and asks the students to identify it. Which of the following correctly identifies the structure marked as "A" in the diagram?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The structure marked A in the diagram is Agger nasi.
• The agger nasi is a ridge or bony prominence on the lateral wall of the nasal cavity. It is part of the ethmoid bone and plays a role in the structure of the nasal passages.

Other Options:
• Option B: Bulla ethmoidalis - The bulla ethmoidalis is a rounded bony structure within the ethmoid bone. It is located in the middle and superior part of the nasal cavity and contains air cells. It contributes to the complexity of the nasal passages.
• Option C: Concha bullosa - A concha bullosa is a condition where one of the nasal conchae, usually the middle turbinate, is enlarged due to the presence of air cells within it. This can sometimes cause nasal obstruction or sinus-related symptoms.
• Option D: Fovea ethmoidalis - The fovea ethmoidalis is a depression or concavity on the ethmoid bone that articulates with the cribriform plate of the ethmoid bone. This region is important because it houses the olfactory bulb, which is responsible for the sense of smell.

stem: All the following are the components of the Thoracoscore, except
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Complication of surgery is not a component of the Thoracoscore.
• The Thoracoscore is a risk prediction model for in-hospital mortality after thoracic surgery.
It is calculated based on the following nine variables:
• Age
• Sex
• American Society of Anesthesiologists (ASA) score
• Performance status
• Dyspnea score
• Priority of surgery (elective/emergency)
• Procedure class (pneumonectomy/other)
• Diagnosis group (benign/malignant)
• Comorbidity score
• The complication of surgery is not a factor that can be predicted before the surgery, so it is not included in the Thoracoscore.

Other Options:
• Option A: Priority of surgery is included in the Thoracoscore.
• Option B: Performance status is included in the Thoracoscore.
• Option C: ASA grading is included in the Thoracoscore.

stem: Which is not a branch of the facial artery in the face?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The mental artery is not a branch of the facial artery in the face.
• Instead, it is a branch of the inferior alveolar artery, which is a branch of the maxillary artery.
• The mental artery supplies blood to the chin and lower lip. It anastomoses with its controlateral fellow and with the mental branch of the inferior alveolar artery.

Other Options:
• Option A: The lateral nasal artery is indeed a branch of the facial artery. It supplies the dorsum and alae of the nose. It anastomoses with its controlateral fellow.
• Option C: The superior labial artery is a branch of the facial artery. It supplies the upper lip. It also anastomoses with its contralateral fellow.
• Option D: The inferior labial artery is also a branch of the facial artery. It mainly supplies the inferior labial glands. It then anastomoses with its controlateral fellow and with the mental branch of the inferior alveolar artery.

stem: A patient was admitted with stab injury to the lower chest region. He was found to have low pulses and his blood pressure has improved after giving intravenous fluids. The chest X-ray was taken and there were clear lungs fields. The next appropriate step would be
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• E-FAST stands for extended focused assessment with sonography for trauma.
• It is a bedside ultrasound examination that can be used to quickly assess for injuries to the heart, lungs, and abdomen in trauma patients.
• In this case, the patient has low pulses and his blood pressure has improved after giving intravenous fluids. This suggests that he may have a hemothorax or pericardial tamponade.
• Given the clear lung fields, cardiac tamponade must be strongly suspected and evaluated rapidly.
• E-FAST can be used to quickly diagnose these conditions and guide the next steps.

Other Options:
• Option A: Chest X-ray is not as sensitive as E-FAST for detecting fluid, and inserting a chest tube blindly is not indicated yet without identifying the exact pathology.
• Option C: CECT chest is more sensitive than E-FAST for detecting injuries to the heart, lungs, and abdomen, but it is also more time-consuming and expensive.
• Option D: CECT abdomen is more sensitive than E-FAST for detecting injuries to the heart, lungs, and abdomen, but it is more time-consuming and expensive.

stem: A 27-year-old male was admitted with a 2 cm thyroid nodule. The thyroidectomy was done in this patient and the HPE picture is given below. The most likely diagnosis would be:
subject: Pathology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Papillary thyroid carcinoma is the most common type of thyroid cancer.
• It is typically well-differentiated, which means that it looks similar to normal thyroid tissue under a microscope.
• Papillary thyroid carcinomas are often small and slow-growing, and they have a good prognosis.
• In the HPE picture, the tumor cells are well-differentiated and have papillary architecture. This is most consistent with a diagnosis of papillary thyroid carcinoma.

Other Options:
• Option A: Toxic nodular goitre is a condition in which there are one or more nodules in the thyroid gland that produce excess thyroid hormone. This can lead to symptoms such as weight loss, anxiety, and heat intolerance. Toxic nodular goitres are typically benign, but they can sometimes become cancerous.
• Option B: Medullary thyroid carcinoma is a rare type of thyroid cancer that arises from the C cells of the thyroid gland. C cells produce calcitonin, a hormone that helps to regulate calcium levels in the blood. Medullary thyroid carcinomas are often aggressive and can spread to other parts of the body.
• Option D: Follicular thyroid carcinoma is another type of thyroid cancer that arises from the follicular cells of the thyroid gland. Follicular cells produce thyroglobulin, a protein that stores thyroid hormone. Follicular thyroid carcinomas are typically slow-growing and have a good prognosis.

stem: In the CT image given below, cystic lesion along with supreasellar calcification can be observed. This is suggestive of
subject: Radiology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Craniopharyngiomas are benign tumors that typically occur in children and young adults.
• They are often cystic and calcified, which is what is seen in the CT image.
• Other features of craniopharyngiomas on CT scan include:
• Suprasellar location
• Sellar enlargement
• Posterior displacement of the optic chiasm
• Irregular borders

Other Options:
• Option A: Astrocytomas are the most common type of brain tumor, but they are typically solid and do not calcify.
• Option B: Meningiomas are typically located in the meninges, which are the membranes that surround the brain and spinal cord. They are often calcified, but they are typically not cystic.
• Option D: Oligodendrogliomas are typically located in the frontal lobes of the brain. They are often solid and do not calcify.

stem: In a patient with achalasia, the following will be reduced
options: NO and VIP in lower esophagus
subject: Physiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Achalasia is a disorder of the esophagus that is characterized by the inability of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) to relax properly.
• This causes food to back up into the esophagus, which can lead to a number of symptoms, including difficulty swallowing, chest pain, and regurgitation.
• The LES is a muscular valve that separates the esophagus from the stomach.
• It is normally closed to prevent food and stomach acid from backing up into the esophagus.
• However, in patients with achalasia, the LES is constantly contracted, which prevents food from passing into the stomach.
• Nitric oxide (NO) and vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP) are neurotransmitters that play a role in relaxing the LES and allowing the passage of food into the stomach.
• In achalasia, the levels of NO and VIP can be reduced, contributing to the dysfunction of the LES and difficulty in swallowing.

Other Options:
• Option A: In achalasia, the LES tone is increased, causing it to remain contracted and not relax properly during swallowing.
• Option B: Due to the lack of relaxation of the LES in achalasia, the diameter of the esophagus can be increased.
• Option C: The pressure in the esophagus can be increased due to the lack of relaxation of the LES in achalasia.

stem: A 6 year old male child was admitted with acute liver failure. All the following are included in the King's college criteria, except
options: Jaundice < 7 days before the development of encephalopathy
subject: Pediatrics, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Jaundice < 7 days before the development of encephalopathy is not included in the King's College criteria. The other options are all included in the criteria.
• The King's College criteria are used to assess the prognosis of patients with acute liver failure. They are based on the following factors:
• Age < 11 years or > 40 years
• Serum bilirubin > 300 µmol/L
• INR > 6.5
• Duration of jaundice before onset of encephalopathy > 7 days
• Creatinine > 300 µmol/L
• Grade III or IV hepatic encephalopathy

Other Options:
• Option A: Age < 11 years - This is included in the King's College criteria. Younger age can be a favorable factor for a liver transplant.
• Option C: INR > 6.5 - This is included in the King's College criteria. An elevated INR indicates impaired blood clotting function, which is a sign of severe liver dysfunction.
• Option D: Bilirubin > 300 µmol/L - This is included in the King's College criteria. Elevated bilirubin levels are indicative of liver dysfunction and are considered in assessing the severity of acute liver failure.

stem: The apparent volume of distribution of a drug is very high (6L/kg). True regarding the distribution of that drug?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• The high apparent volume of distribution indicates the presence of drugs in organs and tissue.
• The apparent volume of distribution is a theoretical volume occupied by the total amount of the drug in the body relative to its plasma concentration considering the body as a homogenous compartment.
• Vd = drug administered / plasma concentration
• A low volume of distribution indicates that the drug is extensively bound to plasma proteins and is largely restricted to the vascular compartment.
• Drugs sequestered in other tissues may have more volume of distribution than Total body water.

Other Options:
• Option A: A large value of Volume of distribution indicates that a larger quantity of the drug is present in extravascular tissue, not bound to plasma proteins.
• Option B: Drugs confined to the vascular compartment usually show a low volume of distribution.
• Option D: Both options A & B are not correct.

stem: In a 12 year-old male child, there was selective mutism. What is the most likely cause?
subject: Psychiatry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Selective mutism is a type of childhood anxiety disorder characterised by a consistent failure to speak in specific social situations where there is an expectation to speak such as in school or social gatherings, despite the ability to speak in other settings.
• Children with selective mutism may feel a strong fear or discomfort in these situations, leading to their inability to speak.
• This may develop earlier but may only be noticeable and apparent in early adolescence, such as in this child who is 12 years old.
• Management involves psychological evaluation and therapy for the child to overcome this social anxiety.

Other Options:
• Option A: Childhood psychosis is not a common cause of selective mutism, and it is more associated with severe disturbances in thinking and perception.
• Option B: Hyperkinetic disorder or Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) may have symptoms of impulsivity and hyperactivity but would not typically cause selective mutism.
• Option C: Childhood depression can cause emotional and social difficulties, but it is less likely to lead to selective mutism compared to anxiety disorders.

stem: Which among the following statements is correct about steroid-dependent nephritis in a child?
options: Sibling should not be given OPV
Give all immunisation according to schedule
Pneumococcal vaccine should be given before starting treatment
subject: Pediatrics, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Steroid-dependent nephritis is a condition where the kidneys are inflamed and require treatment with steroids.
• One important consideration for individuals with this condition is their weakened immune system due to the steroid medication.
• This makes them susceptible to infections. Live vaccines, which contain a weakened form of a live virus, can potentially cause infection in these individuals.
• Therefore, killed or inactivated vaccines are recommended instead, as they pose no risk of infection due to the dead virus.

Other Options:
• Option B: Sibling should not be given OPV: While it's important to consider the vaccination needs of individuals in close contact with someone with a weakened immune system, the recommendation specifically for siblings of someone with steroid-dependent nephritis and the type of vaccine (OPV-Oral Polio Vaccine) is not supported by the provided information. OPV is a live attenuated vaccine, and its use in individuals with immunosuppression or close contact with such individuals may pose a risk.
• Option C: Give all immunizations according to schedule: While a regular immunization schedule may be followed in some cases, it's crucial to adapt it to the specific needs of individuals with steroid-dependent nephritis. This means prioritizing inactivated vaccines and delaying or avoiding live vaccines.
• Option D: Pneumococcal vaccine should be given before starting treatment: Although the timing of pneumococcal vaccination in relation to steroid treatment might vary, there isn't a clear consensus on whether it should be administered before starting treatment.

stem: In a 10 year-old female child, there was a history of dribbling of urine but the voiding pattern was found to be normal. What is the most probable diagnosis?
subject: Pediatrics, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Ureterocele is a congenital anomaly where the end of the ureter balloons or swells as it enters the bladder.
• This can cause dribbling and other urinary symptoms, such as frequent urinary tract infections, urolithiasis and lower abdominal pain.
• However, the voiding pattern appears to be normal. This condition is commonly linked to duplex systems, especially in females.
• Management includes endoscopic roofing procedure.

Other Options:
• Option B: Grade 4 VUR refers to the backflow of urine from the bladder into the ureters, at times up into the kidneys. It can cause recurrent UTIs, increased frequency and urgency. Dribbling may occur in severe cases but is not the primary characteristic symptom of VUR.
• Option C: Ectopic ureteral orifice refers to a ureter that doesn't open into the bladder at the normal location but instead connects to another part of the urinary tract. This presents with urinary incontinence, increased frequency, recurrent UTIs. While it can cause dribbling, it is apparent much earlier in life and is often more severe.
• Option D: A congenital megaureter is a condition where a portion of the ureter is significantly enlarged. This can cause urinary obstruction, UTIs, abdominal pain and discomfort. Dribbling without other significant symptoms is less common in this condition.

stem: The following X-ray was found in a paediatric case. What is the treatment of choice for this condition?
subject: Pediatrics, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• An X-ray of the skull given shows multiple lytic bone lesions with bevelled edges. They are characteristically "punched-out" or "geographic" as is seen in Langerhans cell histiocytosis.
• Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) is a complex and rare disorder characterised by the abnormal accumulation of Langerhans cells in various tissues and organs of the body. It is seen more commonly in paediatric age groups and can present with a wide range of symptoms depending on the affected organs including bone pain, skin rashes, respiratory problems, among others.
• Vinblastine is a chemotherapeutic agent that is commonly used to treat LCH in children.
• It is often used in combination with other therapies, such as corticosteroids like prednisolone and 6-mercaptopurine, to help control the disease and its associated symptoms. The choice of treatment and the specific regimen varies depending on the extent and severity.

Other Options:
• Option A: Vincristine may sometimes be used in combination with vinblastine, but vinblastine is generally preferred as the primary chemotherapeutic agent in LCH.
• Option C: Methotrexate is not a first-line treatment choice for LCH as is used more frequently in cases where central nervous system involvement is present.
• Option D: Cyclosporine is not a standard first-line treatment for LCH. It may be considered in special cases where other treatments are not effective.

stem: In a 12 year-old male child, there was a palpable mass (depicted below) at the lower end of the femur. What is the most probable diagnosis?
subject: orthopaedics, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• The picture given below shows the distal end of the femur that has been affected by the tumour.
• There is a destruction of the epiphyseal plate with minimal spread into the medullary cavity. The tumour has surpassed the cortex and is a large soft tissue mass with uneven, asymmetrical borders.
• All of these features are consistent with osteosarcoma.
• Osteosarcoma is the most common primary malignant bone tumour in children and adolescents.
• It often presents with a painful mass at the end of long bones, such as the distal femur or proximal tibia.
• A thorough evaluation including imaging studies and a biopsy may be required to confirm the diagnosis and plan further treatment.

Other Options:
• Option A: Chondroblastomas are generally benign bone tumours occurring in the epiphyseal region of long bones. They are unlikely to present as an aggressive, palpable mass such as this.
• Option B: Osteochondromas are benign bone tumours that usually develop on the surface of bones near growth plates. They are not typically associated with the presentation of a painful palpable mass at the distal femur, as is the case here.
• Option D: Giant cell tumours can occur in the epiphyseal regions of long bones but are usually more common in adults. While they can cause pain and localised swelling, osteosarcoma is a more likely diagnosis at this age and with the given clinical picture.

stem: In a 7 year-old child, there was a history of bed-wetting and there were no other associated symptoms. The child was extremely stressed due to bed-wetting. What is the initial and most effective therapy for this child?
options: Rewarding for daytime voluntary urine passing
Classical conditioning with bell and alarm
subject: Pediatrics, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Classical conditioning with bell and alarm is the most effective initial therapy for nocturnal enuresis.
• This child has nocturnal enuresis, as he has bed-wetting at 7 years of age.
• There are no other urinary symptoms, suggesting monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis.
• The bell and pad alarm is a behavioral therapy based on classical conditioning.
• The alarm rings when the child starts passing urine during sleep.
• Over time, the child learns to wake up before or at the start of bladder emptying.
• It has better long-term success and lower relapse rates than drug therapy.

Other Options:
• Option A: Rewarding for daytime voluntary urine passing - Reward-based methods may support behavioral management. However, rewarding daytime urine passing does not directly target night-time bed-wetting. Enuresis alarms are more effective for nocturnal enuresis. Positive reinforcement may be used along with alarm therapy. Hence, this is not the best answer.
• Option C: Imipramine drug therapy - Imipramine can reduce bed-wetting episodes. However, it has a higher relapse rate after discontinuation. It may cause adverse effects such as cardiotoxicity, mood changes, and sleep disturbance. It is not preferred as the initial and most effective therapy. Hence, this option is incorrect.
• Option D: Psychodynamic therapy - Psychodynamic therapy is not the first-line treatment for simple nocturnal enuresis. It may be considered only if significant emotional or psychiatric issues are present. Most children with monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis benefit from behavioral methods. The best initial effective therapy is bell and alarm conditioning. Hence, this option is incorrect.

stem: Identify the equipment used below.
subject: Anaesthesia, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• The above picture shows a High-flow nasal cannula.
• It is an oxygen delivery method that is capable of delivering up to 100% heated, humidified oxygen at a flow rate of up to 60 liters per minute.
• Through the use of a positive-pressure environment, a high-flow nasal cannula reduces nasopharyngeal airway resistance, resulting in enhanced ventilation and oxygenation.
• One of the primary disadvantages is the increased cost of care in comparison to a low flow nasal cannula, the increased complexity and training required to initiate care, decreased mobility, the risk of ineffective sealing of the passageways resulting in air leakage and loss of the positive airway pressure effect, and the potential to delay intubation.
Other Options:
• Option A: Nasal cannulas are the most regularly used in our practice. Patients are usually more at ease with the nasal cannula, but we must consider whether the fraction of inspired oxygen (FIO2) given by the nasal cannula is adequate for the patient. At a flow rate of 6 L/min, the maximum fraction of inspired oxygen (FIO2) given by the nasal cannula is 0.44.


• Option B: Non-invasive positive pressure ventilation is the use of positive pressure to supply oxygen to the lungs without the need for endotracheal intubation. It is used to treat both acute and chronic respiratory failure, but it must be carefully monitored and titrated to ensure success and avoid problems.
• Option D: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) is a type of positive airway pressure in which air is delivered into the airways to maintain a constant pressure to keep the airways open in persons who breathe naturally. PEEP stands for positive end-expiratory pressure, which is the pressure in the alveoli at the conclusion of expiration above atmospheric pressure. CPAP is a method of administering PEEP that also keeps the pressure constant throughout the breathing cycle, during both inspiration and expiration.

stem: A 45-year-old male is found unconscious on the road. A thorough assessment reveals that the patient has a cardiac arrest. If you handle such a patient at the event site, which of the following would be your first step in managing such a patient according to ACLS guidelines?
options: Check carotid pulse, CPR, call for help, shock if available
Call for help, check pulse, do CPR
subject: Medicine, Anaesthesia
explanation:
Solution
• The above clinical scenario is cardiac arrest. The first step in managing such a case will be to call for help, check for a pulse, and start doing CPR.




Other Options:
• Option A: Check carotid pulse, CPR, call for help, shock if available - This option is incorrect because first we need to call for help, then check carotid pulse, if not felt start CPR and give a shock if defibrillator is available.
• Option C: Observe is incorrect because this is a case of cardiac arrest and we need to do an intervention for this case to save the life of this patient.
• Option D: Just CPR is incorrect because this is a tiring process we need the help of another person and intensive care is required as soon as possible.

stem: Viral infection mimicking erythroblastosis fetalis is:
subject: Microbiology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Parvovirus B19 is the smallest virus with the smallest genome that has a special tropism for erythroid progenitor cells in the adult bone marrow and fetal liver.
• It binds to the P antigen in the blood cell culture. It causes aplastic crisis in children with sickle cell anemia.
• The clinical features of rashes especially in the cheeks are characteristic of slapped cheek disease or fifth disease otherwise called as erythema infectiosum in children.

Other Options:
• Option A: Herpes simplex virus - HSV causes oropharyngeal disease, keratitis, fever blisters, gingivostomatitis and genital infections and it is also most commonly seen in adults and immunocompromised individuals.
• Option B: Epstein Barr virus is associated with the following infections/disease: Infectious mononucleosis - Glandular fever, Oral hairy leukoplakia in AIDS, Burkitt's lymphoma, Lymphomas in AIDS and transplant patients, Hodgkin's lymphoma, Nasopharyngeal carcinoma (Especially in chinese), Anaplastic gastric carcinoma, T/NK cell lymphoma, Duncan's syndrome or XLP-X linked lymphoproliferative disorder.
• Option C: Picornavirus - Enterovirus, Rhinovirus, Coxsackie virus comes under this family.

stem: The superior gluteal nerve supplies all of the following structures except:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The superior gluteal nerve supplies all of the structures mentioned in the question except for the piriformis muscle. The superior gluteal nerve arises from the dorsal branches of the L4, L5, and S1 spinal nerve rami. It exits the pelvis through the greater sciatic foramen above the piriformis muscle, and it has superior and inferior branches.
• The structures supplied by the superior gluteal nerve include:
• Gluteus medius: This muscle is responsible for abducting the hip joint and is important for stabilizing the pelvis during walking or standing on one leg.
• Gluteus minimus: Similar to the gluteus medius, it aids in hip abduction and stabilization of the pelvis.
• Tensor fasciae latae: This muscle assists in various hip movements, including flexion, abduction, and medial rotation.
• The piriformis muscle, on the other hand, is supplied by the nerve to the piriformis, which originates from the dorsal branches of the S1 and S2 spinal nerve rami.

Other Options:
• Option A: The gluteus medius muscle is supplied by the superior gluteal nerve. This muscle is located on the lateral side of the hip and plays a crucial role in hip abduction, which is the movement of the leg away from the body's midline. It also helps stabilize the pelvis during activities like walking and standing.
• Option B: Similar to the gluteus medius, the gluteus minimus muscle is supplied by the superior gluteal nerve. This muscle is located beneath the gluteus medius and works together with it to perform hip abduction and medially rotate the thigh. Like the gluteus medius, it also contributes to pelvic stability during movement.
• Option D: The tensor fasciae latae muscle is also supplied by the superior gluteal nerve. This muscle is situated at the upper part of the thigh and assists in various movements of the hip joint, including hip flexion, abduction, and medial rotation. It also plays a role in stabilizing the knee joint and supporting the iliotibial tract.

stem: Which skull foramen is formed by 3 bones?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The foramen lacerum is created by the collaboration of three bones: the petrous part of the temporal bone, the greater wing of the sphenoid bone, and the basilar part of the occipital bone.
• This opening serves as a pathway for the internal carotid artery, which enters the skull through this foramen. Accompanying the artery are venous and sympathetic nerve plexuses.
• Additionally, meningeal branches of the ascending pharyngeal artery and emissary veins from the cavernous sinus pass through this foramen.
• Notably, the pterygoid canal leading to the pterygopalatine fossa can be observed on the anterior margin of the foramen lacerum, and this canal transmits the nerve of the pterygoid canal and associated vessels.

Other Options:
• Option B: Foramen magnum is a large opening formed by the occipital bone that accommodates the passage of the spinal cord into the skull.
• Option C: Foramen rotundum is situated within the greater wing of the sphenoid bone and allows the maxillary nerve (V2) of the trigeminal nerve to pass through.
• Option D: Foramen jugulare is created by the petrous part of the temporal bone and the occipital bone. This foramen facilitates the passage of structures like the internal jugular vein, glossopharyngeal nerve (IX), vagus nerve (X), and accessory nerve (XI).

stem:
What are the derivatives of the marked cells?
1. Odontoblasts
2. Sympathetic chain
3. Melanocytes
4. Nephrogenic cord
5. Bulbus cordis
6. Conotruncal septum
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The cells shown in the image are neural crest cells (NCC) located adjacent to the neural tube (NT). Neural crest cells are multipotent cells that contribute to a variety of tissues and organs. Here's the breakdown of the derivatives of the marked cells:
• Odontoblasts: Neural crest cells contribute to the development of odontoblasts, which are cells that form dentin in teeth.
• Sympathetic chain: Neural crest cells give rise to sympathetic neurons and ganglia, including the sympathetic chain.
• Melanocytes: Neural crest cells are responsible for the formation of melanocytes, which produce melanin and are responsible for skin and hair pigmentation.
• Conotruncal septum: Neural crest cells contribute to the formation of the conotruncal septum in the heart, which helps separate the pulmonary trunk and aorta.
• Some additional points to note: The intermediate mesoderm develops into the genital ridge and nephrogenic cord. However, the nephrogenic cord is not a derivative of neural crest cells.
• The bulbus cordis contributes to the majority of the heart, including the right ventricle and outflow tract. It's derived from the pharyngeal mesoderm and is considered a neural crest derivative.

Other Options:
• Option A: 1, 3, 5, 6. This option includes "Bulbus cordis" (Option 5) which is not a neural crest derivative.
• Option B: 2, 3, 4, 6. This option includes "Nephrogenic cord" (Option 4) which is not a neural crest derivative.
• Option D: 1, 2, 3, 4. This option is missing "Conotruncal septum" (Option 6) and includes "Nephrogenic cord" (Option 4).

stem:
Which of the following muscles form the pelvic diaphragm?
A. Iliococcygeus
B. Pubococcygeus
C. Obturator internus
D. Deep transverse perinei
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The muscles that form the pelvic diaphragm include the iliococcygeus and pubococcygeus muscles.
• The pelvic diaphragm is a muscular partition that separates the pelvic cavity from the perineum below.
• These muscles are part of the levator ani muscle group, which supports the pelvic organs and plays a role in maintaining continence.

Other Options:
• Option B: This option suggests that all four muscles listed form the pelvic diaphragm. However, this is not accurate. While the iliococcygeus and pubococcygeus muscles are indeed part of the pelvic diaphragm, the obturator internus and deep transverse perinei muscles are not part of the pelvic diaphragm. The obturator internus muscle is a lateral rotator of the hip, and the deep transverse perinei muscles are involved in stabilizing the perineum.
• Option C: This option also suggests that the iliococcygeus, pubococcygeus, and deep transverse perinei muscles form the pelvic diaphragm. However, as mentioned earlier, only the iliococcygeus and pubococcygeus muscles are part of the pelvic diaphragm. The deep transverse perinei muscles are not part of the pelvic diaphragm; they contribute to perineal stability.
• Option D: This option includes the iliococcygeus and deep transverse perinei muscles. While the iliococcygeus muscle is indeed part of the pelvic diaphragm, the deep transverse perinei muscles are not. The pelvic diaphragm is primarily composed of the iliococcygeus and pubococcygeus muscles.

stem: A 60 year old lady presented in a state of shock and abdominal distension with a history of peptic ulcers. X ray of chest is given below. Identify the condition.
subject: Surgery, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• The above given patient's information and chest x ray image clearly points towards the diagnosis as perforation peritonitis.
• It occurs when there is a hole or tear in the peritoneum, leading to the leakage of the stomach or intestinal contents into the abdominal cavity.
• This can cause severe inflammation, infection, and shock, and is a medical emergency requiring immediate surgical intervention.
• The presence of air under diaphragm on a chest x-ray is a classic sign of perforation peritonitis, as it indicates the escape of gas from the abdominal cavity into the thorax.

Other Options:
• Option B: Pneumothorax: Involve accumulation of air in the pleural space and would not typically be associated with abdominal distension or peptic ulcer disease.
• Option C: Pleural effusion: It involves accumulation of fluid in the pleural space, and would not cause air under diaphragm on an x-ray.
• Option D: Hydropneumothorax: Involve accumulation of air and fluid in the pleural space and would not typically be associated with abdominal distension or peptic ulcer disease.

stem: Most radiosensitive organ of body is:
subject: Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• The most radiosensitive organ in the body varies depending on the age, gender, and other factors.
• However, in females, the ovaries are considered to be one of the most radiosensitive organs in the body.
• This is because radiation exposure to the ovaries can damage the DNA of the oocytes, which can result in infertility or genetic abnormalities in any offspring.
• The sensitivity of the ovaries to radiation also depends on the stage of the menstrual cycle at the time of exposure.

Other Options:
• Option B: Brain: The brain is also a radiosensitive organ, and exposure to high levels of radiation can cause damage to the brain tissue and increase the risk of developing brain tumors.
• Option C: Lungs: The lungs are also sensitive to radiation exposure, particularly to high energy radiation such as alpha and beta particles. Radiation damage to lung tissue can increase the risk of lung cancer and other respiratory diseases.
• Option D: Spleen: The spleen is relatively less sensitive to radiation compared to the ovaries, brain, and lungs. However, exposure to high levels of radiation can still cause damage to spleen tissue and increase the risk of developing certain types of cancer.

stem: The following appearance as shown below is present in:
subject: Radiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The above given chest x ray image clearly points towards the diagnosis as lung metastasis.
• The typical chest x-ray appearance of lung metastasis can vary depending on various factors such as the size, number, and location of the metastatic lesions.
• Typically, lung metastasis appears as round or oval shaped nodules on the x-ray film, which can range in size from a few millimeters to several centimeters.
• The nodules may be well defined or have an irregular shape, and they can appear as single or multiple lesions.
• In some cases, the nodules may have a central area of necrosis or calcification.
• Additional imaging such as a CT scan may be needed to confirm the diagnosis and determine the extent of the metastatic disease.

Other Options:
• Option B: TB: Chest x-ray may show patchy or nodular opacities in the upper lobes or hilar regions, with cavitation or fibrosis in advanced cases.
• Option C: Bronchogenic CA: Chest x-ray appearance may show a solitary pulmonary nodule, hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy, or a mass with irregular or spiculated margins.
• Option D: Hamartoma: Chest x-ray appearance may show a well-circumscribed, round or oval mass with calcifications, usually in the periphery of the lung.

stem: What is the imaging modality given below?
subject: Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• The above given image shows CT enteroclysis.
• CT enteroclysis is a diagnostic imaging procedure that uses a combination of CT and small bowel contrast agents to visualize the small intestine.
• During the procedure, a nasoenteric tube is inserted into the small intestine and contrast material is administered through the tube.
• The patient then undergoes a CT scan, which produces detailed images of the small bowel.
• CT enteroclysis is often used to diagnose conditions such as Crohn's disease, small bowel tumors, and obstructions in the small intestine.

Other Options:
• Option B: MRI: It is a medical imaging technique that uses a strong magnetic field and radio waves to create detailed images of the body's internal structures.
• Option C: Barium enema: involves administering a contrast material containing barium through the rectum for examining the GI tract.
• Option D: X ray: It is an imaging modality that uses ionizing radiation to create images of various body parts.

stem: Neurotransmitter secreted from the arrow marked area is:
subject: Anatomy, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• The area marked in the given image is substantia nigra, which translates to "dark substance." The neurotransmitter secreted by the substantia nigra is dopamine.
• Substantia nigra is a crucial component of the nigrostriatal pathway, and dopamine produced here plays a significant role in various motor functions.
• Degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra is closely linked to the development of Parkinson's disease, a neurodegenerative disorder characterized by movement impairments.
• Additionally, other dopaminergic pathways include:
• Mesocortical
• Mesolimbic
• Tuberoinfundibular

Other Options:
• Option A: Glutamate is a widely distributed excitatory neurotransmitter in the central nervous system. It plays a crucial role in promoting neuronal excitation and communication between nerve cells. Glutamate is not primarily associated with the substantia nigra; rather, it is involved in various aspects of brain function, including learning, memory, and synaptic plasticity.
• Option B: GABA is the main inhibitory neurotransmitter in the brain. It functions to decrease neuronal excitability and has a calming effect on the nervous system. GABA is essential for maintaining a balance between excitatory and inhibitory signals in the brain. However, it is not produced by the substantia nigra but is present in various other regions of the brain.
• Option D: Acetylcholine is a neurotransmitter that has diverse functions in both the central and peripheral nervous systems. It is associated with motor control, memory, and cognitive functions. While acetylcholine is involved in transmitting signals between neurons and muscle cells at neuromuscular junctions, it is not the neurotransmitter primarily secreted by the substantia nigra.

stem: A fracture at the marked arrow leads to bleeding of which artery:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The area marked by an arrow in the given image is known as the pterion, which is an H-shaped sutural junction between four bones of the skull: the frontal bone, squamous part of the temporal bone, greater wing of the sphenoid bone, and parietal bone.
• The artery that passes in close proximity to this area is the middle meningeal artery.
• The pterion is an important landmark because it overlies the anterior branch of the middle meningeal artery and the lateral fissure of the cerebral hemisphere.
• The bone in this region is relatively thin, and any injury or fracture to the pterion area can result in the rupture of the middle meningeal artery, leading to the formation of an extradural (epidural) hematoma.
• Extradural hematomas occur when blood accumulates between the inner surface of the skull and the dura mater (the outermost layer of the meninges).

Other Options:
• Option B: The deep temporal arteries are branches of the maxillary artery, a branch of the external carotid artery. These arteries supply blood to the temporal region of the head. However, in the context of the question and the marked area (pterion), the bleeding is more closely associated with the middle meningeal artery rather than the deep temporal artery.
• Option C: The anterior cerebral artery is a major blood vessel that supplies blood to the anterior (front) part of the cerebral hemispheres. It plays a crucial role in the circulation to the brain. However, the pterion and the middle meningeal artery are not directly related to the anterior cerebral artery. Bleeding in the pterion area is more likely to involve the middle meningeal artery.
• Option D: The internal carotid artery is a major artery that supplies blood to the brain, face, and eyes. It has significant branches that contribute to various aspects of cerebral circulation. However, the pterion and the middle meningeal artery are not typically associated with bleeding from the internal carotid artery. The focus of bleeding from the pterion is on the middle meningeal artery.

stem: A patient on post-parotid surgery, complaints of weakness of lower lip movement. Which of the following nerve is most likely injured:
subject: Surgery, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• After undergoing post-parotid surgery, if a patient experiences weakness of lower lip movement, it suggests involvement of the lower branch of the facial nerve.
• The cervical branch of the facial nerve is the nerve most likely to be injured in this scenario.
• This branch is responsible for supplying motor innervation to the muscles that control movement of the lower lip.

Other Options:
• Option A: The main trunk of the facial nerve is responsible for innervating the muscles of facial expression. However, the specific complaint of lower lip movement weakness points more towards the lower branches of the facial nerve.
• Option B: The temporal branch of the facial nerve controls muscles that are involved in raising the forehead and eyebrows. This branch is not directly related to lower lip movement.
• Option D: The ansa cervicalis is formed by cervical spinal nerve branches and innervates certain neck muscles like sternohyoid and omohyoid. It is not primarily associated with the muscles responsible for lip movement.

stem: Neurovascular bundle is absent in which compartment of the leg?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• In the leg, the neurovascular bundle refers to the combination of nerves and blood vessels (arteries and veins) that travel together within a specific compartment. In this case, the question is asking about the presence or absence of the neurovascular bundle in different compartments of the leg.
• The posterior superficial compartment of the leg includes muscles like the gastrocnemius, plantaris, and soleus. However, it does not contain a neurovascular bundle.

Contents of the other compartments:
• Anterior compartment: This compartment contains the anterior tibial artery and the deep fibular (peroneal) nerve.
• Posterior deep compartment: This compartment houses the tibial nerve and the posterior tibial vessels in the deep plane.
• Lateral Compartment: In the lateral compartment, you can find the superficial peroneal (fibular) nerve and the peroneal vessel. It's important to note that the peroneal artery is a branch of the posterior tibial artery.

Other Options:
• Option A: The lateral compartment of the leg does contain a neurovascular bundle. Specifically, it contains the superficial peroneal (fibular) nerve and the peroneal vessel. The peroneal artery, a branch of the posterior tibial artery, contributes to the blood supply in this compartment.
• Option C: The posterior deep compartment of the leg does contain a neurovascular bundle. This compartment houses the tibial nerve and the posterior tibial vessels, which are essential for supplying blood and innervation to the deep structures of the leg.
• Option D: The anterior compartment of the leg also contains a neurovascular bundle. It includes the anterior tibial artery and the deep fibular (peroneal) nerve. These structures are responsible for supplying blood and innervation to the muscles in the anterior compartment of the leg.

stem: Decussation of superior cerebellar peduncle occurs at:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The decussation (crossing) of the superior cerebellar peduncle occurs at a specific location within the brainstem.
• The superior cerebellar peduncles are white matter tracts that connect the cerebellum to the midbrain.
• The decussation of these peduncles, where fibers cross from one side to the other, takes place at the level of the midbrain.
• This decussation is an important anatomical feature that allows for coordinated communication between the cerebellum and other parts of the central nervous system.

Other Options:
• Option A: The diencephalon is a region of the brain that includes structures like the thalamus and hypothalamus. However, the decussation of the superior cerebellar peduncle does not occur in the diencephalon. It takes place at a lower level within the brainstem.
• Option C: The pons is a part of the brainstem that connects the midbrain to the medulla oblongata. While there are other decussations and crossings that occur within the pons, the decussation of the superior cerebellar peduncle specifically takes place at the level of the midbrain, not in the pons.
• Option D: The medulla oblongata is the lowest part of the brainstem, connecting to the spinal cord. While there are several decussations that occur within the medulla, the decussation of the superior cerebellar peduncle occurs at a higher level within the brainstem, specifically at the midbrain.

stem: A 40-year-old male presents to neurology OPD with complaints of imbalance when walking with eyes closed and has to lift his foot and stomp on the ground to walk. His examination reveals a loss of fine touch and two-point discrimination. The lesion noted in the medial lemniscus is the continuation of which tract?
subject: Anatomy, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The symptoms described in the patient's presentation, along with the findings of loss of fine touch and two-point discrimination, point towards a lesion involving sensory pathways. The given options refer to different sensory tracts, and the correct answer is: Option B: Fasciculus gracilis.
• The medial lemniscus is a major ascending tract that carries fine touch, vibration, and proprioceptive (position sense) information from the lower half of the body. The fasciculus gracilis is the continuation of the posterior column-medial lemniscus pathway for these sensory modalities from the lower extremities.
• A lesion in the medial lemniscus involving the fasciculus gracilis can result in the symptoms described, such as imbalance when walking with eyes closed and loss of fine touch and two-point discrimination.

Other Options:
• Option A: The spinothalamic tract is responsible for transmitting pain and temperature sensations. It is not directly associated with the loss of fine touch and two-point discrimination mentioned in the patient's symptoms.
• Option C: Spinocerebellar tracts are involved in transmitting proprioceptive information to the cerebellum for coordination of motor movements. Lesions in these tracts might lead to issues with coordination and movement, but they are not specifically related to fine touch and two-point discrimination loss.
• Option D: The spinotectal tract is involved in relaying sensory information related to the orientation of the head and eyes in response to visual and auditory stimuli. It plays a role in reflexive movements of the head and eyes but is not directly associated with the loss of fine touch and two-point discrimination.

stem: Difference between exocrine pancreas and parotid gland is:
options: Presence of serous acini with acidophilic tip
Serous acinus supported by basal lamina
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• One of the key differences between the exocrine pancreas and the parotid gland lies in the presence or absence of striated ducts. The exocrine pancreas lacks striated ducts, which sets it apart from the parotid gland.
• Striated ducts are a characteristic feature of certain salivary glands, including the parotid gland.
• Therefore, the absence of striated ducts in the exocrine pancreas is an important distinguishing factor when comparing these two glands.

Other Options:
• Option A: This option describes a histological feature of certain glands, including the parotid gland. It refers to the presence of serous acini (secretory units) with an acidophilic (eosinophilic) tip. This is characteristic of serous salivary glands like the parotid gland. However, this does not differentiate between the exocrine pancreas and the parotid gland.
• Option C: The presence of a basal lamina, a thin layer of extracellular matrix, is a common structural feature of glandular tissues. Both the exocrine pancreas and the parotid gland have a basal lamina supporting their acinar cells. This feature is not a distinctive difference between the two glands.
• Option D: Apical acinar villi, which refer to projections of the acinar cell apical surface into the lumen of a gland, are a structural feature of certain glandular cells. However, this is not a specific difference between the exocrine pancreas and the parotid gland.

stem: All of the following marked nerves in the given image cause movement of the Eyeball except?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The question is addressing the nerves marked in the image and their involvement in the movement of the eyeball. The correct answer is Option A: A.
• The nerve marked as "A" corresponds to the optic nerve (II CN), which is primarily responsible for transmitting visual information from the retina to the brain. The optic nerve does not directly control the movement of the eyeball; its main function is visual sensation and perception.

Other Options:
• Option B: The nerve marked as "B" corresponds to the oculomotor nerve (III CN). The oculomotor nerve innervates several extraocular muscles responsible for eye movement, including the superior rectus, inferior rectus, medial rectus, and inferior oblique muscles. It does play a role in the movement of the eyeball.
• Option C: The nerve marked as "C" corresponds to the abducens nerve (VI CN). The abducens nerve innervates the lateral rectus muscle, which is responsible for abduction (outward movement) of the eyeball. It does play a role in the movement of the eyeball.
• Option D: The nerve marked as "D" corresponds to the trochlear nerve (IV CN). The trochlear nerve innervates the superior oblique muscle, which is involved in various movements of the eyeball. It does play a role in the movement of the eyeball.

stem: The given structure not found in?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The question is addressing the structure indicated by an arrow in the image and its presence or absence in various anatomical locations. The correct answer is Option B: Epididymis.
• The structure shown in the image is cilia. The epididymis is the structure where sperm are stored and mature in the male reproductive system. However, the epididymis is not lined with ciliated epithelium.
• Instead, it is lined by pseudostratified columnar epithelium with stereocilia. Stereocilia are specialized structures that are not the same as the cilia typically found in other locations.

Other Options:
• Option A: The oviduct (also known as the fallopian tube or uterine tube) is part of the female reproductive system. It is lined with ciliated columnar epithelium, which helps in the transport of eggs (ova) from the ovaries to the uterus.
• Option C: The bronchi are part of the respiratory system and are responsible for carrying air to and from the lungs. The lining of the bronchi contains ciliated columnar epithelium that helps in the movement of mucus and foreign particles out of the respiratory tract.
• Option D: The ependyma is a thin layer of cells that lines the ventricles of the brain and the central canal of the spinal cord. It is involved in producing and circulating cerebrospinal fluid. The ependymal cells are typically ciliated, contributing to the circulation of cerebrospinal fluid.

stem: Which of the following structures contribute behind the marked area?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The question pertains to the anatomical structures that contribute to the area behind the marked structure, which is the pterion. The correct answer is Option B: Lesser Wing of Sphenoid.
• The pterion is an H-shaped sutural junction between multiple bones of the skull, including the frontal bone, squamous part of the temporal bone, greater wing of the sphenoid bone, and parietal bone.
• The structure that contributes to the area behind the pterion is indeed the lesser wing of the sphenoid bone. The lesser wing of the sphenoid bone forms part of the lateral cranial base and plays a role in forming the anterior cranial fossa.

Other Options:
• Option A: The clivus is a sloping surface at the back of the skull that supports the brainstem. However, the clivus is not directly associated with the pterion or the area behind it.
• Option C: The sella turcica is a saddle-shaped depression within the sphenoid bone that holds the pituitary gland. While it is part of the sphenoid bone, it is not specifically behind the pterion.
• Option D: The carotid canal is a passage within the temporal bone through which the internal carotid artery passes. It is located more inferiorly and is not directly related to the area behind the pterion.

stem: Muscle attached to the marked area?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The question is addressing the muscle that is attached to the marked area on the palmar aspect of the first metacarpal. The correct answer is Option C: Opponens Pollicis.
• The opponens pollicis muscle originates from the tubercle of the trapezium and the flexor retinaculum. It inserts into the whole length of the lateral border and adjoining lateral half of the palmar aspect of the first metacarpal bone.
• This muscle is responsible for flexing the metacarpal bone of the thumb and is involved in opposing the thumb to the other digits, allowing for precision grip.

Other Options:
• Option A: The abductor pollicis longus muscle is located on the dorsal (back) aspect of the hand. It is responsible for abducting (moving away) the thumb flexorfrom the hand. It does not have an attachment at the marked area on the palmar aspect of the first metacarpal.
• Option B: The palmar interosseous muscles are located within the palm of the hand and are involved in adduction (bringing together) of the fingers. They do not have a direct attachment to the marked area on the first metacarpal.
• Option D: The flexor pollicis brevis muscle is located on the palmar aspect of the hand and is involved in flexion of the thumb. However, it is not specifically attached to the marked area on the first metacarpal.

stem: Actions of a muscle at the hip attached to the marked area?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The muscle attached to the marked area, which is the gluteal tuberosity, is the gluteus maximus muscle. This muscle plays a significant role in the actions of the hip joint.
• The correct actions associated with the gluteus maximus muscle at the hip joint are:
• 1. Extension: The gluteus maximus muscle is a major extensor of the hip joint. This means it is responsible for moving the thigh backward, such as when standing up from a seated position or climbing stairs.
• 2. Lateral Rotation: The gluteus maximus muscle is also involved in lateral rotation of the hip joint, which refers to the outward rotation of the thigh away from the midline of the body.
• The gluteus maximus muscle is not only involved in these actions but also contributes to powerful abduction of the thigh. Additionally, its insertion into the iliotibial band (IT band) has implications for the stability and function of the hip and knee.

Other Options:
• Option A: While the gluteus maximus muscle does contribute to abduction (moving the thigh away from the midline of the body) and external rotation (outward rotation) of the hip joint, its primary and most powerful actions are extension and lateral rotation, as explained above.
• Option C: The gluteus maximus muscle does perform extension and lateral rotation at the hip joint. However, it is not a primary muscle for adduction (bringing the thigh toward the midline of the body). The adduction of the thigh is primarily performed by the adductor muscles.
• Option D: The gluteus maximus muscle is not a significant muscle for flexion (bending) at the hip joint. Additionally, while abduction is a role of the gluteus maximus, it is not the primary combination of actions.

stem: Identify the marked Foramen in this butterfly shaped bone?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The question involves identifying the specific foramen marked in the image of the butterfly-shaped sphenoid bone. The correct answer is Option A: Foramen Rotundum.
• The structure marked in the image is the foramen rotundum. The foramen rotundum is a foramen located in the sphenoid bone. It is an important anatomical landmark through which the maxillary branch of the trigeminal nerve (V2) passes.
• The trigeminal nerve is the fifth cranial nerve, and its maxillary branch is responsible for carrying sensory information from the midface and maxillary region.

Other Options:
• Option B: The foramen ovale is another opening in the sphenoid bone that is located posterior and lateral to the foramen rotundum. It allows the passage of the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve (V3) as well as blood vessels.
• Option C: The vidian canal (also known as the pterygoid canal or pterygopalatine canal) is a passageway that allows communication between the pterygopalatine fossa and the middle cranial fossa. It is not the specific structure marked in the image.
• Option D: The foramen spinosum is yet another foramen located in the sphenoid bone. It is located lateral to the foramen ovale. The middle meningeal artery passes through the foramen spinosum.

stem: In which of the following lymphoid organ reticular fibers not found?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The thymus, a primary lymphoid organ, is the specific organ in which reticular fibers are not found. Reticular fibers are fine, anastomosing fibers primarily composed of type III collagen.
• They form a network-like structure within various tissues. However, in the thymus, unlike other lymphoid organs, reticular fibers are notably absent.
• Instead, the thymus is characterized by the presence of epithelial cells arranged in a network-like structure that plays a crucial role in the development and maturation of T-lymphocytes (T cells), a key component of the immune system.


• Reticular fibers are found in:
• All Lymphoid Organs: Reticular fibers are present in lymphoid organs such as the spleen, lymph nodes, and bone marrow (with the exception of the thymus). These fibers contribute to the structural framework of these organs and play a role in supporting immune functions.
• Glands, Liver, and Kidneys: Reticular fibers are also found in certain glandular tissues, the liver, and the kidneys. They contribute to the supportive framework of these organs.
• Basement Membrane of Epithelia: Reticular fibers are not limited to organs; they are also present in the basement membrane of epithelial tissues. The basement membrane is an essential structural component that separates epithelial tissues from underlying connective tissues.
• Surrounding Smooth Muscle and Nerve Cells: Reticular fibers provide structural support to smooth muscle cells and nerve cells. They help maintain the organization of these tissues.
Other Options:
• Option B: The spleen is a secondary lymphoid organ that filters blood, removes damaged blood cells, and plays a role in immune responses. It contains reticular fibers that provide structural support for its organization.
• Option C & Option D: Both lymph nodes and bone marrow utilize reticular fibers to form the scaffolding that supports the high density of cells in these primary and secondary lymphoid organs.

stem: A 35-year-old came to the orthopedic OPD saying he is unable to abduct upper limb, as a junior resident which root value do you think is involved:
subject: orthopaedics, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• In this scenario, the patient's complaint of being unable to abduct the upper limb points to a specific root value that is likely involved in the condition. Abduction of the arm involves the action of the deltoid muscle.
• The deltoid muscle is innervated by the axillary nerve, and its motor fibers arise from the C5 and C6 spinal nerve roots.
• The inability to abduct the arm in this patient is most likely due to a lesion involving the C5 root. The axillary nerve, which innervates the deltoid muscle, receives contributions from the C5 and C6 spinal nerve roots. However, since the patient's main symptom is the inability to abduct the arm, it is reasonable to suspect that the C5 root, which provides the primary innervation to the deltoid muscle, is involved.

Other Options:
• Option B: While the axillary nerve also receives innervation from the C6 root, the primary function of the axillary nerve related to arm abduction aligns more with the C5 root involvement. Option A (C5) is a more accurate fit for the patient's symptom.
• Option C: The C4 spinal nerve root is not directly related to the motor innervation of the deltoid muscle responsible for arm abduction. Therefore, it is less likely to be the root value involved in the patient's inability to abduct the arm.
• Option D: The C7 spinal nerve root is not directly associated with the motor innervation of the deltoid muscle responsible for arm abduction. It's important to consider the specific muscle function and nerve root distribution when identifying the likely cause of the patient's symptom.

stem: Identify the marked structure which does not form the laryngeal skeleton?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The structure marked as 'A' in the image is the hyoid bone.
• The hyoid bone is not a part of the laryngeal skeleton.
• Instead, it is located in the neck and serves as a unique bone that supports the base of the tongue and provides attachment points for various muscles involved in swallowing and speech.
• Unlike other cartilaginous structures like the thyroid cartilage, cricoid cartilage, and epiglottis, the hyoid bone is not directly involved in the formation of the laryngeal skeleton.

Other Options:
• Option B: The structure marked as 'B' in the image is the thyroid cartilage. The thyroid cartilage is a prominent cartilaginous structure that forms a part of the laryngeal skeleton and is commonly known as the "Adam's apple."
• Option C: The structure marked as 'C' in the image is the epiglottis. The epiglottis is a cartilaginous flap-like structure that is also a part of the laryngeal skeleton. Its primary function is to cover the entrance to the trachea during swallowing to prevent food and liquids from entering the airway.
• Option D: The structure marked as 'D' in the image is the cricoid cartilage. The cricoid cartilage is a ring-like cartilaginous structure that forms a part of the laryngeal skeleton, situated just below the thyroid cartilage.

stem: Which of the following muscles are not used in overhead abduction of arms?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The pectoralis major muscle is mainly involved in adduction and medial rotation of the arm, rather than the abduction of the arm.
• During overhead abduction of the arms, the primary muscles involved are the deltoid, supraspinatus, and trapezius.
• The first 15° of abduction is initiated by the supraspinatus muscle. From 15° to 90°, the middle fibers of the deltoid muscle play a crucial role in abduction.
• Beyond 90° the movement of the shoulder girdle is influenced by the action of the trapezius and serratus anterior muscles.
• It's important to understand that the pectoralis major muscle's main actions are adduction and medial rotation of the arm. It doesn't have a significant role in the process of overhead abduction.

Other Options:
• Option A: The serratus anterior muscle is involved in the movement of the scapula (shoulder blade) and plays a role in protraction and rotation of the scapula. However, it is also involved in upward rotation of the scapula, which aids in overhead movements like abduction of the arms. During the process of overhead abduction, the serratus anterior muscle helps stabilize the scapula and ensures proper positioning of the shoulder joint. While it is not the primary muscle responsible for the movement, it does contribute to the overall stability and coordination of the shoulder girdle.
• Option B: The trapezius muscle is a large muscle that covers the upper back and neck region. It has three parts: upper, middle, and lower. The middle fibers of the trapezius play a role in scapular retraction and elevation. While the trapezius muscle is not a primary muscle for the abduction of the arms, it does have an indirect role in stabilizing the scapula and providing support during overhead movements. The trapezius, along with the serratus anterior, contributes to the proper positioning of the scapula during various arm movements.
• Option C: The deltoid muscle is a powerful muscle that covers the shoulder joint. It has three distinct parts: anterior, middle, and posterior. The middle part of the deltoid is heavily involved in the abduction of the arm. It is responsible for lifting the arm away from the body, especially during the initial phase of overhead abduction. As the arm is raised beyond 90 degrees, the deltoid continues to play a significant role in controlling the movement and maintaining stability of the shoulder joint. The deltoid is a key muscle in achieving full range of motion during abduction of the arm.

stem: Bleeding as shown in the image is due to which of the following vessels?
subject: Anatomy, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The image depicts a subdural hemorrhage, which is a collection of blood between the dura and the arachnoid layers of the meninges.
• This type of hemorrhage is commonly caused by the shearing or tearing of cortical bridging veins that drain blood from the brain's surface into the dural sinuses.
• These veins traverse the subdural space and are vulnerable to injury, leading to bleeding in this location.

Other Options:
• Option B: Middle Meningeal Artery - The rupture of the middle meningeal artery results in an extradural hemorrhage, where blood accumulates between the dura mater and the inner surface of the skull. This type of bleeding creates a hematoma between the dura and the skull, often associated with head trauma.
• Option C: Lenticulostriate Artery - Rupture of the lenticulostriate artery, which is a small vessel that supplies blood to deep brain structures, would cause an intraparenchymal hemorrhage. This means the bleeding occurs directly within the brain tissue itself, rather than between the dura and arachnoid layers.
• Option D: Vertebral Artery - Damage to the vertebral artery, typically through dissection, can lead to subarachnoid hemorrhage or, in some cases, intraparenchymal hemorrhage. Subarachnoid hemorrhage involves bleeding into the cerebrospinal fluid-filled space surrounding the brain.

stem: Which structure is supplied by the nerve causing this elevation?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The Risorius muscle is supplied by the facial nerve (also known as cranial nerve VII), which causes the elevation marked in the image.
• The facial nerve is responsible for controlling the muscles of facial expression.
• The Risorius muscle is part of the buccolabial muscle group and is involved in actions like grinning and laughing.
• It functions as an elevator, retractor, and evertor of the upper lip.
• It has peripheral attachments to the zygomatic arch, parotid fascia, and fascia over the mastoid process.

Other Options:
• Option A: The lateral rectus is an extraocular muscle responsible for outward movement of the eye, a movement known as abduction. This muscle is primarily involved in controlling eye movements and is innervated by the abducens nerve (cranial nerve VI).
• Option B: The superior oblique is an extraocular muscle responsible for downward and outward movement of the eye, a movement known as depression and abduction. It also helps in intorsion (rotation of the top of the eyeball towards the nose). The superior oblique muscle is innervated by the trochlear nerve (cranial nerve IV).
• Option D: The levator palpebrae superioris is an extraocular muscle responsible for elevating the upper eyelid, a movement that opens the eye. This muscle helps to lift the eyelid and allows for exposure of the eye's surface. The levator palpebrae superioris muscle is innervated by the oculomotor nerve (cranial nerve III).

stem: The rupture of penile urethra does not cause extravasation of blood into which of the following spaces?
subject: Anatomy, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The rupture of the penile urethra can cause extravasation of blood into various spaces, but extravasation into the ischiorectal fossa is highly unlikely due to the anatomical relationships involved.
• The ischiorectal fossa is located laterally and inferiorly to the anal canal, bounded by the pelvic diaphragm superiorly, the obturator internus muscle laterally, and the external anal sphincter muscle inferiorly.
• The rupture of the penile urethra is more likely to lead to blood extravasation into spaces such as the superficial perineal pouch and the deep perineal pouch. Therefore, it's a better answer considering the anatomical considerations.

Other Options:
• Option A: Superficial Perineal Pouch - Blood could potentially extravasate into the superficial perineal pouch due to penile urethral rupture. This pouch is located between the perineal membrane and Colles' fascia.
• Option B: Deep Perineal Pouch - Similar to the superficial perineal pouch, blood could also extravasate into the deep perineal pouch due to penile urethral rupture.
• Option D: Skin around the Scrotum - Blood could potentially travel through the fascial layers and lead to swelling around the scrotum in the event of penile urethral rupture.

stem:
A patient was diagnosed to have pulmonary artery hypertension. The following findings will predict >10% mortality within one year.
1. Pericardial effusion
2. Recurrent syncope attack
3. Enlarged RA > 26cm2
4. Ve /VCO2 slope <36
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Let's examine each finding and its significance in predicting mortality in PAH patients:
• Pericardial effusion: Pericardial effusion refers to the accumulation of fluid in the pericardial sac surrounding the heart. In PAH, pericardial effusion can be a sign of increased stress on the cardiovascular system. It may indicate advanced disease and is associated with a higher risk of mortality.
• Recurrent syncope attack: Recurrent syncope attacks, which involve sudden loss of consciousness or fainting, can be indicative of severe PAH and inadequate blood flow to the brain. They suggest a poor prognosis and an increased risk of mortality.
• Enlarged right atrium (RA) > 26cm2: An enlarged right atrium (>26cm2) is a marker of significant right-sided heart dysfunction in PAH. It indicates the progression of the disease and is associated with a higher mortality rate.
• Ve/VCO2 slope <36: The Ve/VCO2 slope is a measure of ventilation-perfusion mismatch during exercise. In PAH, a higher Ve/VCO2 slope is associated with poorer prognosis and increased mortality. However, the question specifies a Ve/VCO2 slope <36 as a predictor of mortality, which contradicts the established understanding. This appears to be an error or a misinterpretation.
• Based on the analysis, the correct option is Option A: 1, 2, 3. Pericardial effusion, recurrent syncope attack, and an enlarged right atrium (>26cm2) are the findings that predict a mortality rate of more than 10% within one year for patients with pulmonary artery hypertension.

Other Options:
• Option B: This option includes the incorrect finding of a Ve/VCO2 slope <36, which contradicts the expected correlation with mortality. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
• Option C: This option includes the incorrect finding of a Ve/VCO2 slope <36. Hence, this option is incorrect as well.
• Option D: This option includes all the findings mentioned in the previous options, including the incorrect finding of a Ve/VCO2 slope <36. Thus, this option is also incorrect.

stem: Which of the following lobes of the lung is affected when there is aspiration in supine position?
options: Upper part of right inferior lobe
subject: Anatomy, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• When a person aspirates while in a supine position (lying flat on their back), the most common area affected is the upper part of the right inferior lobe of the lung.
• This is because in the supine position, the upper part of the right inferior lobe is more dependent, allowing aspirated material to pool and block airways in that area.

Other Options:
• Option A: The lingula is part of the left lung, not the right lung. Aspiration in the supine position would generally affect the right lung more prominently.
• Option C: The right superior lobe is less likely to be affected by aspiration in the supine position compared to the right inferior lobe. Aspiration tends to pool in the lower parts of the lung due to gravity.
• Option D: The right middle lobe is also less likely to be affected by aspiration in the supine position compared to the upper part of the right inferior lobe. Aspiration is more likely to occur in the dependent regions of the lung.

stem: The cells marked in the image given below will be involved in the inhibition of the following structure:
options: Anterior horn cells of the spinal cord
subject: Anatomy, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• The flask-shaped neuron marked in the image is a Purkinje cell located in the cerebellar cortex. Purkinje cells are a type of inhibitory neuron found in the cerebellum.
• The question asks which structure is inhibited by the marked Purkinje cell. Among the given options, the correct answer is Option C: Deep cerebellar nuclei.
• The deep cerebellar nuclei are a group of nuclei located deep within the cerebellum.
• They receive input from various sources, including Purkinje cells, and play a crucial role in the coordination and modulation of motor functions.
• Purkinje cells provide inhibitory signals to the deep cerebellar nuclei, helping to fine-tune and regulate motor output.
• Therefore, the cells marked in the image, which are Purkinje cells, are involved in the inhibition of the deep cerebellar nuclei. The correct option is Option C.

Other Options:
• Option A: The vestibular nuclei are a group of nuclei located in the brainstem, specifically in the medulla and pons. They receive input from the vestibular system, which is responsible for detecting changes in head position and movement, and play a key role in maintaining balance and coordinating eye and head movements. While Purkinje cells in the cerebellum do receive input from the vestibular system, they are not directly involved in inhibiting the vestibular nuclei. Therefore, Option A is not the correct answer.
• Option B: The lenticular nucleus, also known as the lentiform nucleus, is a structure located in the basal ganglia. It consists of two main components: the putamen and the globus pallidus. The basal ganglia are involved in motor control and play a role in regulating movement initiation, coordination, and suppression. While the cerebellum and basal ganglia interact in motor control, Purkinje cells in the cerebellum are not directly involved in inhibiting the lenticular nucleus. Therefore, Option B is not the correct answer.
• Option D: The anterior horn cells, also known as the motor neurons, are located in the anterior horns of the spinal cord gray matter. These motor neurons transmit signals from the central nervous system to the skeletal muscles, controlling voluntary muscle movements. While the cerebellum does interact with the spinal cord in motor coordination, Purkinje cells in the cerebellum are not directly involved in inhibiting the anterior horn cells. Therefore, Option D is not the correct answer.

stem: The vein marked in the image given below has this embryological origin:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The structure marked in the given image is the azygos vein, which develops from the supracardinal vein.
• Development: The azygos venous system arises from the supra cardinal veins embryologically. The right supra cardinal vein becomes the azygos vein, while the left supra cardinal vein becomes the hemiazygos vein.
• During embryonic development, a network of veins known as the cardinal veins develops on each side of the embryo.
• The cardinal veins consist of the cranial cardinal vein and the caudal cardinal vein.
• The cranial cardinal vein eventually forms the superior vena cava, while the caudal cardinal vein gives rise to the inferior vena cava.
• The supracardinal veins are a set of paired veins that develop parallel to the cardinal veins.
• The supracardinal veins undergo extensive remodeling and give rise to various veins in the adult circulatory system, including the azygos vein.
• The azygos vein is a large vein that drains deoxygenated blood from the posterior thoracic and abdominal wall.
• It forms on the right side of the vertebral column and ascends towards the superior vena cava. The azygos vein is derived from the right supracardinal vein during embryonic development.
• Therefore, the correct answer is Option D: Supracardinal vein.

Other Options:
