3. Hydrofluoric acidC. Fomepizole
4. ValproateD. Calcium Gluconate
subject: Pharmacology, FMT
explanation:
Solution
• The correct, life-saving matching of these specific, highly toxic drugs/poisons with their exact pharmacological antidotes is Option A: 1-B, 2-C, 3-D, 4-A.
• Here is the exhaustive, detailed explanation of the biochemical rationale behind each critical pairing:
• 1. Heparin - Antidote: Protamine (Match B). Heparin is a highly negatively charged, strongly acidic glycosaminoglycan used extensively as a potent intravenous anticoagulant to prevent deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism. In the catastrophic event of a massive heparin overdose causing life-threatening hemorrhage, the specific, rapid-acting antidote is Protamine sulfate. Protamine is a highly basic, strongly positively charged protein derived originally from salmon sperm. When administered intravenously, the strongly positive protamine molecules instantly seek out and fiercely bind to the strongly negative heparin molecules through intense electrostatic interaction. This forms a stable, completely inert, and biologically inactive salt complex, instantaneously neutralizing heparin's anticoagulant effects and rescuing the patient from bleeding out.
• 2. Ethylene glycol - Antidote: Fomepizole (Match C). Ethylene glycol is a sweet-tasting, highly toxic industrial solvent universally found in automotive antifreeze. When ingested, ethylene glycol itself is relatively non-toxic; the lethal danger arises when the hepatic enzyme alcohol dehydrogenase relentlessly metabolizes it into extremely toxic downstream metabolites, primarily glycolic acid (causing profound, deadly high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis) and oxalic acid (which precipitates with calcium to form massive, destructive calcium oxalate crystals that physically shred the renal tubules, causing catastrophic acute kidney failure). The life-saving, highly specific antidote is Fomepizole. Fomepizole works by acting as a highly potent, competitive inhibitor of the alcohol dehydrogenase enzyme. By completely blocking this exact enzyme, it totally halts the conversion of ethylene glycol into its toxic metabolites, allowing the parent, non-toxic ethylene glycol compound to be safely and slowly excreted unchanged by the kidneys.
• 3. Hydrofluoric acid - Antidote: Calcium Gluconate (Match D). Hydrofluoric (HF) acid is an exceptionally terrifying and uniquely hazardous industrial chemical used in glass etching, rust removal, and petroleum refining. Unlike other corrosive acids that cause immediate, obvious surface burns, HF acid rapidly and insidiously penetrates deep, deep into the tissues without initial pain. Once deep inside the body, the incredibly reactive fluoride ions wildly scavenge and violently bind to all available systemic calcium and magnesium. This massive, sudden depletion causes instantaneous, profound, and often lethal hypocalcemia and hypomagnesemia, leading to catastrophic, intractable cardiac arrhythmias and cardiac arrest, alongside severe, agonizing deep tissue liquefactive necrosis and bone destruction. The absolute, life-saving antidote is massive quantities of Calcium Gluconate, applied copiously as a topical gel, injected deeply into the affected tissues, or infused intravenously. The exogenous calcium rapidly bonds with the rampaging free fluoride ions, precipitating them out as completely insoluble, harmless calcium fluoride (CaF2), instantly neutralizing the toxicity and preventing lethal hypocalcemia.
• 4. Valproate - Antidote: Levocarnitine (Match A). Valproate (valproic acid) is a broad-spectrum, highly effective, widely prescribed antiepileptic and mood-stabilizing drug. In cases of a massive, intentional, or accidental overdose, valproate toxicity can manifest as severe central nervous system depression, coma, and a highly specific, very dangerous complication: valproate-induced hyperammonemic encephalopathy. Massive doses of valproate deplete the body's natural stores of carnitine and completely inhibit the urea cycle in the liver, leading to a massive, toxic accumulation of ammonia in the blood, which crosses the blood-brain barrier and causes severe brain swelling and coma. The specific, life-saving antidote for this unique complication is Levocarnitine (L-carnitine). Administering massive, supplemental doses of Levocarnitine rapidly replenishes the depleted carnitine stores, re-activates mitochondrial beta-oxidation, and kickstarts the urea cycle, facilitating the rapid clearance of the toxic ammonia from the blood and reversing the deadly encephalopathy.

Other Options:
• Option B: 1-A, 2-B, 3-C, 4-D. This option catastrophically incorrectly matches each drug with a completely different, pharmacologically useless antidote. Giving levocarnitine for a heparin bleed would do absolutely nothing to stop the hemorrhage, and giving protamine to a patient dying of antifreeze poisoning would be equally useless.
• Option C: 1-D, 2-C, 3-B, 4-A. This option also wildly swaps the matches. Giving calcium gluconate to a patient bleeding out from heparin would not stop the hemorrhage, and giving protamine for a hydrofluoric acid burn would allow the patient to die from rapid, lethal hypocalcemia.
• Option D: 1-B, 2-D, 3-A, 4-C. This option again lethally rearranges the matches. Giving calcium gluconate for ethylene glycol poisoning would not stop the metabolism of the poison into toxic acids, and giving fomepizole for a massive valproate overdose would fail to reverse the deadly hyperammonemic encephalopathy.

stem: A 15 year old female came to OPD with the complaints of cough and sore throat. She was prescribed an antibiotic following which she came back with the following symptom as shown in the image. Which drug would have caused it?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Amoxicillin is an antibiotic commonly used to treat bacterial infections. However, some individuals may be allergic to this medication. When a person is allergic to amoxicillin, they can develop various symptoms, including skin rashes. These rashes can vary in appearance and severity, ranging from mild redness to more extensive and severe eruptions.
• Amoxicillin and ampicillin are aminopenicillins. The incidence of skin rashes appears to be highest following the use of ampicillin, at about 9%.
• In the scenario you described, the 15-year-old female developed rashes after being prescribed Amoxicillin for her cough and sore throat. This is a classic example of an allergic reaction to the medication.

Other Options:
• Option A: Fexofenadine is an antihistamine commonly used to relieve allergy symptoms like runny nose, sneezing, and itching. It is not typically associated with causing skin rashes as a side effect. It works by blocking the action of histamine, which is released during allergic reactions.
• Option B: Salbutamol (also known as albuterol) is a bronchodilator used to treat conditions like asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). It helps to relax the muscles in the airways and improve breathing. While it can have side effects like tremors or a rapid heartbeat, it is not known to cause skin rashes.
• Option D: Corticosteroids are anti-inflammatory medications that are often used to treat a variety of conditions, including allergic reactions, asthma, and inflammatory skin conditions. While long-term or high-dose use of corticosteroids can sometimes lead to skin issues, they are not commonly associated with causing the type of skin rashes that are typically seen as an allergic reaction.

stem: The disruption of which of the following is responsible for watery diarrhea in cholera caused by Vibrio?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• In cholera caused by Vibrio cholerae, watery diarrhea is primarily a result of the disruption of zonula occludens.
• Zonula occludens, also known as tight junctions, are specialized structures found between epithelial cells in the intestine that play a crucial role in maintaining the integrity and impermeability of the intestinal barrier.
• Here's how Vibrio cholerae and its toxins affect zonula occludens and lead to watery diarrhea:
• 1. Vibrio cholerae produces cholera toxin, which consists of A and B subunits.
• 2. The B subunits of cholera toxin bind to specific receptors on the surface of intestinal epithelial cells, particularly GM1 ganglioside receptors.
• 3. Once bound, the toxin is internalized into the enterocytes.
• 4. Within the enterocyte, the A subunit of cholera toxin, specifically the A1 fragment, activates adenylate cyclase.
• 5. Activation of adenylate cyclase leads to an increase in intracellular levels of cyclic AMP (cAMP).
• 6. Elevated cAMP levels result in the disruption of zonula occludens or tight junctions between epithelial cells.
• 7. The disruption of tight junctions allows for the unregulated efflux of water and electrolytes into the intestinal lumen, leading to profuse, watery diarrhea.
• This disruption of zonula occludens is a key mechanism by which cholera toxin causes the characteristic watery diarrhea seen in cholera infections. It results in the loss of water and electrolytes from the body, which can lead to severe dehydration and electrolyte imbalances if not promptly treated.

Other Options:
• Option B: Hemidesmosomes are cell adhesion structures that anchor epithelial cells to the underlying basement membrane. They are essential for maintaining the structural integrity of tissues. Disruption of hemidesmosomes would not directly lead to watery diarrhea; instead, it might result in the detachment of epithelial cells from the basement membrane.
• Option C: Intercalated discs are specialized cell junctions found in cardiac muscle tissue. They play a crucial role in coordinating the contraction of cardiac muscle cells. Disruption of intercalated discs would primarily affect the function of the heart, not the intestines, and would not lead to watery diarrhea.
• Option D: The macula densa is a specialized structure in the kidney's distal convoluted tubule that plays a role in regulating renal blood flow and glomerular filtration rate. Disruption of the macula densa function would impact kidney function but would not directly cause watery diarrhea.

stem: A 60-year-old farmer comes to the dermatological clinic complaining of a large discharging growth on his right foot. The farmer is unsure of when the growth started but says it's been getting worse over the past 1 year. On examination, a large tumor-like mass is seen with multiple discharging sinuses and fistulae. Pus, containing several granules, is seen oozing out of the wound, and despite the appearance of the lesion, the farmer complains of very little pain in his foot. A picture of his foot is given below. What is the diagnosis of this farmer?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Given the farmer's presentation and the characteristic features described, mycetoma is the most likely diagnosis.
• Mycetoma is a chronic and granulomatous infection that often affects the foot or hand, leading to tumefactions, deformities, and draining sinuses. It's caused by various microorganisms, including fungi (eumycetomas) and bacteria (actinomycetomas). In this case, the farmer's condition is likely mycetoma caused by Eumycetoma.
• The characteristic features of mycetoma include:
• 1. Traumatic inoculation: It typically occurs after traumatic inoculation of the organism into the subcutaneous tissue, often from soil or vegetable sources, such as thorns or splinters.
• 2. Draining sinuses: The infection results in the formation of draining sinuses that discharge fungal colonies known as grains or granules.
• 3. Painlessness: Despite the alarming appearance of the lesion, mycetoma is often surprisingly painless.
• The laboratory diagnosis involves the direct examination of pus collected from draining sinuses or biopsy material, where the presence of grains is diagnostic. Additionally, culture of the organism can be performed.
• Treatment of mycetoma, especially eumycetoma, is challenging and usually involves surgical debridement or excision along with chemotherapy. Actinomycetomas tend to respond well to rifampicin in combination with sulfonamides or cotrimoxazole.

Other Options:
• Option B: Sporotrichosis is a fungal infection caused by the Sporothrix schenckii fungus. It typically presents as nodules or ulcers on the skin, often in a linear pattern along lymphatic vessels. This is known as the "rose-gardener's disease" because it can be acquired from handling plants and soil. Unlike mycetoma, sporotrichosis doesn't typically result in large discharging growths with multiple sinuses and fistulae.
• Option C: Melanoma is a type of skin cancer that originates from melanocytes, the cells that produce melanin. Melanomas often appear as pigmented lesions on the skin, which can be brown, black, or multicolored. They are known for their irregular borders and asymmetrical shape. While melanoma can present with ulceration and bleeding, it doesn't commonly produce the chronic discharging growths seen in mycetoma.
• Option D: Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) is the most common form of skin cancer. It usually appears as a pearly or waxy bump on the skin, often with visible blood vessels. BCCs are typically slow-growing and rarely metastasize. They may occasionally ulcerate or bleed but don't typically resemble the chronic and granular appearance associated with mycetoma.

stem: The given life cycle belongs to which of the following organisms?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• The given life cycle belongs to Leptospira, the causative organism of leptospirosis. This disease is transmitted through contact with the urine or blood of infected animals, particularly rodents like rats, which are common reservoirs. The illness has two phases, acute leptospiremic and resolving immune phase.
• Clinical manifestations can range from mild flu-like symptoms to severe forms like Weil's disease, characterized by hemorrhage, jaundice, and acute kidney injury.
• Treatment typically involves antibiotics like IV penicillin or IV ceftriaxone.

Other Options:
• Option B: Hantaviruses are a group of viruses that are primarily transmitted to humans through contact with the urine, droppings, or saliva of infected rodents, particularly deer mice. They can cause diseases like Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome (HPS) and Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome (HFRS). The life cycle shown in the image is not related to hantaviruses.
• Option C: Yersinia pestis is the bacterium responsible for causing bubonic, septicemic, and pneumonic plagues. Plague is typically transmitted through the bite of infected fleas or handling of infected animals. The life cycle in the image does not correspond to the transmission cycle of Yersinia pestis.
• Option D: Salmonella typhi is the bacterium responsible for causing typhoid fever, which is transmitted through the consumption of contaminated food or water. The life cycle shown in the image is unrelated to the transmission of Salmonella typhi.

stem: Identify the organism shown in this image.
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• The given image depicts Ascaris lumbricoides, commonly known as the roundworm. It is the most prevalent human helminth parasite found worldwide.
• Ascaris lumbricoides primarily resides in the small intestine, with the majority (85%) in the jejunum and the rest (15%) in the ileum. These adult worms are cylindrical in shape, with pointed anterior ends.
• Humans are the natural host for Ascaris, and there is no need for an intermediate host in its life cycle.
• Infection occurs when individuals consume food or water contaminated with feces containing Ascaris eggs. These eggs contain infective rhabditiform larvae, leading to the transmission of the parasite.

Other Options:
• Option B: Strongyloides stercoralis is a different parasitic worm. It is commonly known as the threadworm or thread-like worm. Unlike Ascaris, Strongyloides stercoralis has a unique life cycle that involves both free-living and parasitic stages. It can cause strongyloidiasis in humans, primarily affecting the intestines and sometimes spreading to other organs. However, the image shown does not represent Strongyloides stercoralis.
• Option C: Enterobius vermicularis, commonly referred to as the pinworm, is another parasitic worm. It is known for causing enterobiasis, a common intestinal infection in humans. Pinworms typically infect the colon and rectum, causing itching around the anus. While Enterobius vermicularis is a common parasite, it differs in appearance from Ascaris, and the image does not match its characteristics.
• Option D: Ancylostoma duodenale is a type of hookworm that infects humans. Hookworms attach to the intestinal wall and feed on blood, leading to a condition known as hookworm infection. They are primarily found in tropical and subtropical regions. The image provided does not resemble Ancylostoma duodenale, as hookworms have distinct features different from Ascaris.

stem: Which of the following disease has the highest mortality once clinically manifested?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Rabies is a viral disease that is virtually 100% fatal once clinical symptoms appear. Here's how it spreads and why it has such a high mortality rate:
• Rabies is a zoonotic disease, meaning it can be transmitted from animals to humans. Once the rabies virus enters the peripheral neurons (nerve cells), it travels via retrograde fast axonal transport towards the central nervous system (CNS), which includes the spinal cord and brainstem. This process occurs at a remarkable speed of up to 250 mm/day.
• Once within the CNS, the virus quickly spreads to different regions of the central nervous system. It primarily infects neurons but may also rarely infect astrocytes, a type of brain cell.
• From the CNS, rabies virus exhibits centrifugal spread, moving along sensory and autonomic nerves to various parts of the body, including:
• 1. Salivary glands: The virus replicates in the acinar cells of the salivary glands and is secreted in the saliva of rabid animals, making saliva a potential source of transmission to humans.
• 2. Adrenal glands
• 3. Skin
• Due to the rapid and widespread infection of the central nervous system and the subsequent spread to critical areas of the body, rabies becomes almost uniformly fatal once it manifests clinically. This high mortality rate makes rabies a particularly dangerous disease.

Other Options:
• Option A: Nipah virus is a zoonotic virus that can cause severe respiratory and neurological symptoms in humans. While Nipah virus infection can be fatal, it doesn't have the same near-100% fatality rate as rabies. Mortality rates for Nipah virus infection vary, but they are generally lower than rabies. The fatality rate can range from 40% to 75%, depending on the outbreak and the healthcare measures in place.
• Option C: Ebola virus disease is a highly contagious and deadly viral illness. It is known for causing severe hemorrhagic fever. The mortality rate for Ebola can vary by outbreak, but it is typically high, ranging from 25% to 90%. While Ebola is a deadly disease, it does not have a near-100% fatality rate like rabies.
• Option D: Zika virus infection is primarily transmitted through Aedes mosquitoes. It can cause various symptoms, including fever, rash, joint pain, and conjunctivitis. Most Zika virus infections are mild, and fatalities are extremely rare. Zika is not associated with a high mortality rate when compared to rabies or diseases like Ebola.

stem:
In which of the following situations will you use a sanitizer?
A. Before examining the patient
B. After examining the patient
C. After touching the patient's surroundings
D. Hands are visibly soiled
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• In a healthcare setting, it is essential to maintain proper hand hygiene to prevent the spread of infections. Option A correctly identifies the situations where you should use a sanitizer:
• 1. Before examining the patient: Using a sanitizer before examining a patient helps reduce the risk of transferring germs from your hands to the patient, ensuring a safer interaction.
• 2. After examining the patient: After completing the examination, using a sanitizer is crucial to prevent potential cross-contamination to other patients or surfaces within the healthcare facility.
• 3. After touching the patient's surroundings: Healthcare environments can harbor various pathogens on surfaces. Using a sanitizer after touching the patient's surroundings helps maintain a clean and sanitary environment, reducing the risk of infection transmission.

Other Options:
• Option B: correctly includes using a sanitizer before and after examining the patient (situations A & B). However, it omits using a sanitizer after touching the patient's surroundings (situation C), which is also an important practice for infection control in healthcare settings.
• Option C: includes all situations, including using a sanitizer when hands are visibly soiled (situation D). While sanitizers are effective for routine hand hygiene, when hands are visibly soiled, it's essential to wash them with soap and water for a thorough cleaning. Therefore, including situation D is not entirely accurate, as visible soiling requires handwashing, not just sanitizer use.
• Option D: includes using a sanitizer when hands are visibly soiled (situation D) and omits the use of a sanitizer before and after examining the patient (situations A & B). This option is not entirely accurate, as it leaves out crucial moments for sanitizer use in a healthcare setting.

stem:
Galactomannan in serum is seen in:
1. Invasive aspergillosis
2. Systemic candidiasis
3. Mucormycosis
4. Cryptococcosis
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Galactomannan is a crucial diagnostic marker for identifying invasive (deep-seated) mycoses caused by Aspergillus species. Aspergillus is a type of fungus, and Galactomannan is a component found in its cell wall.
• To diagnose invasive aspergillosis, medical professionals use the Galactomannan assay, which can detect Galactomannan in either serum or bronchoalveolar lavage.
• This test is particularly valuable when dealing with specific patient groups, such as hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients or individuals with hematologic malignancies, as these populations are at higher risk for invasive aspergillosis.
• Additionally, other diagnostic features of invasive aspergillosis may include:
• Positive culture from the affected organ
• Aspergillus antigen detection of galactomannan
• Observing narrow, septate hyphae with branching at approximately 45° and conidiophores
• The presence of a "halo sign" on a CT scan

Other Options:
• Option B: This option suggests that galactomannan in serum is seen in both invasive aspergillosis and systemic candidiasis. However, while galactomannan is indeed associated with invasive aspergillosis, it is not a characteristic marker for systemic candidiasis. Systemic candidiasis is usually diagnosed through blood cultures or other specific tests for Candida species.
• Option C: According to this option, galactomannan in serum is seen in invasive aspergillosis and mucormycosis. Galactomannan is a marker for invasive aspergillosis, as explained earlier. However, it is not typically associated with mucormycosis. Mucormycosis is caused by a different group of fungi called Mucorales, and its diagnosis relies on different methods.
• Option D: This option proposes that galactomannan in serum can be seen in invasive aspergillosis, systemic candidiasis, and mucormycosis. While galactomannan is indeed linked to invasive aspergillosis, it is not a diagnostic marker for systemic candidiasis or mucormycosis. Systemic candidiasis is usually diagnosed through blood cultures, and mucormycosis requires specific diagnostic tests for Mucorales fungi.

stem: A 28 year old male admitted with a history of altered sensorium and hydrophobia. A suspicion of rabies was made and you want to diagnose the condition. You have taken corneal scrapings from the patient and which of the following tests will you do to confirm your diagnosis.
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• When suspecting a diagnosis of rabies, confirming it is crucial. One method to confirm rabies is by performing a viral culture from corneal scrapings. Rabies is caused by the rabies virus, and culturing the virus from an appropriate specimen can provide definitive evidence of infection.
• In cases of suspected rabies, multiple tests are often performed in succession or simultaneously for the most accurate diagnosis. The Direct Fluorescent Antibody (DFA) test is typically the initial step, followed by virus isolation through culture. Brain and saliva specimens are considered the best for virus isolation, but corneal smears and centrifuged urine can also be used.

Other Options:
• Option B: Serological tests like indirect immunofluorescence are less sensitive than viral culture for diagnosing rabies. They may not provide the definitive confirmation required.
• Option C: Negri bodies, characteristic intracellular inclusions seen in rabies, are typically found in brain tissue, especially in pyramidal cells of Ammon's horn and Purkinje cells of the cerebellum. Testing for Negri bodies is not commonly done on corneal scrapings.
• Option D: While real-time PCR is highly sensitive and specific for detecting rabies virus RNA, it is not considered a confirmatory test on its own. It can detect the virus in various samples, but for definitive confirmation, viral culture is often preferred.

stem: A 25 year old male with history of fever and enlarged lymph nodes and sore throat was suspected to have infectious mono-nucleosis and was ordered a Paul Bunnel test. Which of the following is the principle of the test?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• The Paul Bunnell test is a diagnostic test used to detect infectious mononucleosis, a viral infection commonly caused by the Epstein-Barr virus (EBV). The principle of this test is based on the detection of heterophile antibodies.
• Heterophile antibodies are antibodies that can agglutinate (clump together) red blood cells (RBCs) from different animal species. In the Paul Bunnell test, sheep erythrocytes are typically used for agglutination.
• Here's how the test works:
• If a person has infectious mononucleosis caused by EBV, their immune system produces heterophile antibodies, specifically IgM heterophile antibodies.
• These IgM heterophile antibodies have the unique property of being able to agglutinate (clump together) sheep RBCs.
• Therefore, when a patient's serum containing these heterophile antibodies is mixed with sheep RBCs in the Paul Bunnell test, agglutination occurs.

Other Options:
• Option A: This option does not describe the principle of the Paul Bunnell test. The test is based on the detection of heterophile antibodies, not complement-mediated cell damage.
• Option B: While agglutination is indeed a part of the Paul Bunnell test, the primary principle is the detection of heterophile antibodies that cause this agglutination.
• Option D: The Paul Bunnell test does not primarily involve a neutralization reaction. It focuses on the agglutination of sheep RBCs due to the presence of heterophile antibodies.

stem: Urine LAM is used for the diagnosis of:
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Urine lipoarabinomannan (LAM) assay is used for the diagnosis of tuberculosis (M. tuberculosis).
• The urine Lipoarabinomannan (LAM) assay is a diagnostic test used to detect tuberculosis (TB) caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. LAM is a component found in the cell wall of mycobacteria, including M. tuberculosis. This test is particularly valuable in diagnosing TB in individuals with HIV infection and low CD4 counts because it can help detect TB even when other tests might be less reliable.

Other Options:
• Option B: This option is incorrect. Urine LAM is not used to diagnose leprosy. Leprosy is caused by Mycobacterium leprae, but a different set of diagnostic tests is used for it.
• Option C: Urine LAM is not used to diagnose Mycoplasma infections. Mycoplasma is a different group of bacteria and requires different diagnostic methods.
• Option D: Urine LAM is not used for the diagnosis of Listeria monocytogenes infections. Listeriosis, caused by Listeria monocytogenes, is diagnosed through other laboratory tests, not the LAM assay.

stem: 1,3 beta D-glucan is helpful for identification of?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• 1,3 beta D-glucan is helpful for the identification of Invasive candidiasis (invasive fungal infections). The 1,3 beta D-glucan assay detects the presence of (1-3)-B-d-glucan (BDG), which is a cell wall polysaccharide found in most fungi, including Candida species. Therefore, it's a valuable tool for diagnosing invasive candidiasis.

Other Options:
• Option B: 1,3 beta D-glucan is not typically used to identify Rhizopus infections. Rhizopus is a genus of molds, and this test is more commonly associated with yeast-like fungi like Candida.
• Option C: The explanation mentioned that Cryptococci do not contain (1-3)-B-d-glucan, so this test is not helpful in identifying Cryptococcus infections.
• Option D: Mucormycosis is caused by fungi from the Zygomycetes group, as mentioned in the explanation. The test is not particularly useful for identifying these types of fungal infections, including Mucormycosis.

stem: Relapse is seen in?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Relapse is seen in cases of Plasmodium vivax and ovale.
• In malaria, relapses occur when the parasite reactivates after an initial infection. This happens in the cases of Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium ovale.
• Here's how it works:
• In Plasmodium vivax and ovale infections, there are two types of sporozoites: some multiply inside hepatic (liver) cells to form schizonts, and others persist as dormant hypnozoites.
• The true relapse is caused by the activation of these hypnozoites. When they become active, they develop into schizonts, release merozoites, and infect red blood cells (RBCs), leading to a clinical relapse.
• However, in the cases of Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium malariae, the initial tissue phase disappears completely, and no hypnozoites are formed. Therefore, relapses are not seen in these types of malaria infections.

Other Options:
• Option A: This option is partially correct. Plasmodium vivax does indeed cause relapses, as explained earlier. However, Plasmodium falciparum typically does not cause relapses. It's known for causing severe and often life-threatening malaria but doesn't involve the formation of hypnozoites that lead to relapses.
• Option B: This option is partially correct as well. Plasmodium ovale can indeed cause relapses due to the presence of hypnozoites, as explained earlier. Plasmodium falciparum, on the other hand, is not associated with relapses.
• Option D: This option is partially correct. Plasmodium vivax can cause relapses due to hypnozoites, as mentioned earlier. Plasmodium malariae, similar to Plasmodium falciparum, typically does not cause relapses.

stem: What is the incubation period of lymphogranuloma venereum?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• The incubation period of lymphogranuloma venereum (LGV) ranges from 10-30 days.
• Lymphogranuloma venereum is a sexually transmitted infection caused by specific serovars (L1, L2, and L3) of the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis.
• The incubation period is the time between exposure to the bacteria and the development of symptoms.
• During this incubation period, the bacteria enter the body and begin to multiply, eventually causing symptoms such as painful inguinal lymphadenopathy (swollen lymph nodes in the groin area).
• LGV typically has a longer incubation period compared to some other sexually transmitted infections, which is why the correct answer is 10-30 days (Option C).

Other Options:
• Option A: This option suggests an incubation period of 1 to 5 days for lymphogranuloma venereum. However, this is not accurate.
• LGV typically has a longer incubation period, as mentioned earlier, ranging from 10 to 30 days. An incubation period of 1 to 5 days would be more characteristic of certain other sexually transmitted infections but not LGV.
• Option B: This option proposes an incubation period of 7 to 10 days for LGV. While this is closer to the correct range than Option A, it still falls short of the actual incubation period.
• LGV generally takes longer to develop, with an incubation period of 10 to 30 days.
• Option D: This option suggests a longer incubation period of 30 to 60 days for LGV. This range includes the correct incubation period, which is 10 to 30 days. However, it also extends beyond the upper limit of the typical LGV incubation period.
• While some cases may have a longer incubation period, the majority fall within the 10 to 30 days range.

stem: Which is the agent used to treat the infection caused by the cestode egg in the image?
subject: Microbiology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• The egg in the image is of Hymenolepis nana (dwarf tapeworm), and it is treated by praziquantel (5-10 mg/kg once).
• Here's some additional information:
• Hymenolepis nana is a parasitic tapeworm that can infect the human intestine. It is one of the smallest tapeworms that can affect humans.
• Praziquantel is the drug of choice for treating infections caused by tapeworms, including Hymenolepis nana. It is highly effective against a wide range of tapeworm species.
• The egg of Hymenolepis nana is distinctive in its appearance. It is oval, colorless, non-bile stained, and has a single shell with two distinct membranes.
• The inner membrane has two small knobs or poles from which 4 to 8 polar filaments arise and spread out between the two membranes. It is embryonated, and the oncosphere inside has six hooks (three pairs of hooklets).


Other Options:
• Option B: Ivermectin is an antiparasitic medication, but it is primarily used to treat infections caused by various types of roundworms and certain types of mites.
• It is not the first-line treatment for cestode infections like Hymenolepis nana.
• Praziquantel is the preferred choice for cestode infections.
• Option C: Albendazole is another antiparasitic drug, and it is effective against a broad spectrum of parasites, including many types of worms.
• While it can be used to treat some cestode infections, praziquantel is generally the preferred treatment for Hymenolepis nana due to its specific effectiveness against tapeworms.
• Option D: Diethylcarbamazine is used to treat infections caused by filarial worms, which are a different type of parasitic worm compared to cestodes like Hymenolepis nana.
• It is not the appropriate treatment for cestode infections.

stem: Human herpesvirus 8 is associated with all except:
subject: Microbiology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Human herpesvirus 8 (HHV8), also known as Kaposi sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (KSHV), is associated with several diseases, but it is not associated with Adult T-cell leukemia.
• Adult T-cell leukemia is caused by the Human T-cell lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1).
• Here are the associations of HHV8 (KSHV) with diseases:
• 1. Kaposi Sarcoma: HHV8 is strongly associated with Kaposi sarcoma, a cancer that affects the skin and mucous membranes. It is particularly common in individuals with weakened immune systems, such as those with HIV/AIDS.
• 2. Castleman's Disease: HHV8 is also linked to Castleman's disease, specifically the multicentric variant. Castleman's disease is a rare lymphoproliferative disorder.
• 3. Primary Effusion Lymphoma: HHV8 is associated with Primary Effusion Lymphoma (PEL), a type of non-Hodgkin lymphoma that typically occurs in the body cavities.

Other Options:
• Option A: This option is correct. Human herpesvirus 8 (HHV8), also known as Kaposi sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (KSHV), is strongly associated with Kaposi sarcoma.
• This cancer primarily affects the skin and mucous membranes and is often seen in individuals with compromised immune systems, such as those with HIV/AIDS.
• Option B: This option is correct as well. HHV8 is linked to Castleman's disease, particularly the multicentric variant.
• Castleman's disease is a rare lymphoproliferative disorder that can affect the lymph nodes and other lymphoid tissues.
• Option D: This option is correct too. HHV8 (KSHV) is associated with Primary Effusion Lymphoma (PEL), which is a type of non-Hodgkin lymphoma that typically occurs in the body cavities, such as the pleural, pericardial, or peritoneal spaces.

stem: The following test is positive for identification of:
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• The image shows the germ tube test, which is used for identifying Candida albicans.
• Here's how the germ tube test works:
• When yeast cells of Candida albicans are incubated in serum maintained at 37°C for about 90 minutes, they will begin to form germ tubes. This is known as the Reynolds-Braude phenomenon.
• This test is a characteristic feature of Candida albicans and is one of the methods used to distinguish it from other Candida species. Candida albicans is the most common and pathogenic species among the Candida group.
• It's worth noting that this test is also positive for Candida dubliniensis, which is closely related to Candida albicans.

Other Options:
• Option B: The germ tube test, as described in the question, is typically negative for Candida glabrata.
• Candida glabrata is a species of Candida that does not form germ tubes.
• Instead, it is known for its distinctive characteristics, such as resistance to antifungal drugs.
• Option C: Similar to Candida glabrata, the germ tube test is usually negative for Candida parapsilosis.
• Candida parapsilosis is another Candida species with its own set of identifying features.
• It is often associated with infections in hospitalized patients.
• Option D: The germ tube test is typically negative for Candida tropicalis as well.
• Candida tropicalis is another species of Candida that does not exhibit germ tube formation.
• It is known for causing a variety of fungal infections in humans.

stem: Choose the correct statement regarding CMV.
options: Ganciclovir resistance is due to mutation in UL97 phosphotransferase gene
CMV can be cultured in vitro in any mammalian cell
Commonest manifestation of CMV associated disease is watery diarrhea
UL54 mutated CMV strains do not show cross resistance to Foscarnet
subject: Microbiology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Ganciclovir resistance is due to mutation in UL97 phosphotransferase gene
• This statement is correct. Ganciclovir is an antiviral medication used to treat infections caused by Cytomegalovirus (CMV). Resistance to ganciclovir, particularly in CMV, often occurs due to mutations in the UL97 phosphotransferase gene of the virus.
• The UL97 gene encodes a protein kinase that is crucial for the activation of ganciclovir within CMV-infected cells. Mutations in this gene can reduce the ability of the kinase to phosphorylate ganciclovir, which is a necessary step for the drug to become active against the virus. As a result, this mutation can lead to ganciclovir resistance, making the medication less effective in treating CMV infections.

Other Options:
• Option B: This statement is incorrect. Human Cytomegalovirus (HCMV) does not grow readily in any mammalian cell. It primarily replicates in vitro in human fibroblasts, although it can be isolated from other cell types within the host.
• Option C: This statement is incorrect. The commonest manifestation of CMV infection is often subclinical in healthy individuals, meaning they don't display noticeable symptoms.
• In immunocompromised individuals, CMV can cause various symptoms and diseases, but watery diarrhea is not the most common presentation.
• Option D: This statement is incorrect. UL54 mutations in CMV can indeed confer resistance not only to ganciclovir but also to other antiviral drugs like cidofovir and foscarnet.
• Therefore, UL54 mutations can lead to cross-resistance to multiple antiviral agents, making treatment more challenging when resistance develops.

stem: Solvent/Detergent (SD) plasma destroys lipid-enveloped viruses. So, on SD plasma transfusion, which of the following infection is likely to occur?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Solvent/Detergent (SD) plasma treatment is designed to remove lipid-enveloped viruses from blood products. This treatment is effective against viruses that have a lipid envelope, which is a layer of lipids (fats) that surrounds the viral particle. Enveloped viruses are more vulnerable to disruptions in their lipid envelopes, which makes them more susceptible to destruction by SD treatment.
• Hepatitis A Virus (HAV) is a non-enveloped virus, meaning it lacks a lipid envelope. As a result, SD plasma treatment is not effective against HAV. Therefore, if SD plasma containing HAV were transfused, it is unlikely to cause an HAV infection because HAV is resistant to this particular method of viral inactivation.

Other Options:
• Option A: HIV is an enveloped virus, which means it has a lipid envelope surrounding its viral particle. Solvent/Detergent (SD) plasma treatment is effective against enveloped viruses like HIV.
• If SD-treated plasma containing HIV were transfused, there is a risk of HIV infection if the treatment process was not effective in completely eliminating the virus.
• However, modern blood screening methods are highly effective at detecting and removing HIV from blood products, reducing this risk significantly.
• Option C: Similar to HIV, hepatitis B virus (HBV) is also an enveloped virus. SD plasma treatment is effective against enveloped viruses.
• Transfusion of SD-treated plasma containing HBV could potentially lead to HBV infection if the treatment process was not completely effective.
• However, as with HIV, blood screening and safety measures have reduced the risk of HBV transmission through blood products.
• Option D: Hepatitis C virus (HCV) is another enveloped virus. SD plasma treatment is effective against enveloped viruses.
• Transfusion of SD-treated plasma containing HCV could potentially result in HCV infection if the treatment process did not completely eliminate the virus.
• Again, blood screening and safety measures have reduced the risk of HCV transmission through blood products.

stem: A cook prepares sandwiches for 10 people going for a picnic. Eight out of them develop severe gastroenteritis within 4-6 hrs of consumption of the sandwiches. It is likely that on Investigations the cook is found to be the carrier of:
subject: Microbiology, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• In this scenario, the rapid onset of severe gastroenteritis (within 4-6 hours) after consuming sandwiches strongly suggests Staphylococcus aureus as the causative agent.
• Staphylococcus aureus is a bacterium that produces toxins, particularly enterotoxins, which are heat-stable toxins.
• When food contaminated with Staphylococcus aureus is left at room temperature (around picnic conditions) for some time, the bacteria can multiply and produce these toxins in the food.
• When individuals consume the contaminated food, they ingest these pre-formed toxins. The toxins can lead to rapid symptoms of food poisoning, including severe gastroenteritis, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, typically within 4-6 hours after ingestion.
• The presence of eight out of ten people developing symptoms after consuming the sandwiches strongly indicates a common source of contamination, which could be the cook.

Other Options:
• Option A: Salmonella typhi is the bacterium responsible for causing typhoid fever. Typhoid fever typically has a longer incubation period than what is described in the question.
• It usually takes around 6-30 days after consuming contaminated food or water for symptoms to appear. The symptoms of typhoid fever include high fever, abdominal pain, and a slow onset of gastrointestinal symptoms.
• Option B: Vibrio cholera is the bacterium responsible for causing cholera. Cholera is characterized by profuse, watery diarrhea and can lead to severe dehydration.
• Cholera outbreaks are often associated with contaminated water sources and tend to have a shorter incubation period than typhoid fever but still longer than the rapid onset described in the question.
• Option C: Entamoeba histolytica is a protozoan parasite responsible for causing amoebic dysentery.
• The symptoms of amoebic dysentery, which include diarrhea with blood and mucus, typically do not have such a rapid onset. It usually takes longer for symptoms to develop after exposure to the parasite.

stem: Which of the following infection presents with mucus in stools and abdominal pain?
subject: Microbiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Mucus in stools and abdominal pain are common clinical features of infection due to Entamoeba. Specifically, this refers to Entamoeba histolytica, which is the causative agent of amoebiasis or amebic dysentery.
• Entamoeba histolytica is a protozoan parasite that can infect the intestines. In cases of intestinal amebiasis, it often presents with the following symptoms:
• Mucus in stools: The presence of mucus in stools is a characteristic feature of amoebic dysentery. The mucus may be accompanied by blood.
• Abdominal pain: Patients infected with Entamoeba histolytica may experience abdominal discomfort or pain, particularly in the lower abdomen.

Other Options:
• Option A: Giardia is often associated with watery diarrhea, and while it can cause abdominal discomfort, it typically does not lead to the same degree of mucus in stools as seen with amoebiasis.
• Option C: Campylobacter infections are characterized by fever, abdominal pain, and watery diarrhea. Stools may contain white blood cells (leukocytes) and blood.
• Option D: Staphylococcus aureus causes staphylococcal food poisoning, which is toxin-mediated and leads to symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. While it involves the gastrointestinal system, it does not typically produce mucus in stools.

stem: A patient's plasma has high levels of interferon beta. He most likely has an infection due to:
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• High levels of interferon-beta in a patient's plasma are most likely indicative of an infection due to a virus.
• Interferon-beta (IFN-β) is a type of interferon, which is a group of signaling proteins released by cells in response to infections, particularly viral infections.
• Interferons play a crucial role in the antiviral immune response. They help to inhibit the replication of viruses within infected cells and also stimulate the immune system to combat viral infections.
• Among the different types of interferons (IFN-α, IFN-β, IFN-γ), IFN-β is particularly associated with the body's response to viral infections.
• When a patient's plasma has high levels of IFN-β, it suggests that the patient's immune system is responding to a viral infection, as interferons are typically produced in response to viral pathogens.

Other Options:
• Option B: In general, high levels of interferon-beta are not typically associated with bacterial infections. The primary role of interferons, including IFN-β, is to respond to viral infections.
• Bacteria and viruses are different types of pathogens, and the immune response to them involves different mechanisms.
• Bacterial infections typically trigger other components of the immune system, such as phagocytes (white blood cells that engulf and destroy bacteria), complement proteins, and specific antibodies.
• Option C: Similarly, high levels of interferon-beta are not typically associated with fungal infections. Fungal infections elicit different immune responses, including the activation of immune cells like macrophages and neutrophils.
• The immune response to fungal pathogens involves different signaling pathways compared to the response to viruses.
• Option D: Mycoplasma infections, including those caused by organisms like Mycoplasma pneumoniae, are primarily bacterial infections. While mycoplasmas are among the smallest known free-living organisms and have some unique characteristics, they are still bacteria.
• Therefore, high levels of interferon-beta are not typically associated with mycoplasma infections either. As with other bacterial infections, the immune response to mycoplasma involves different mechanisms.

stem: Which of the following is the most potent stimulator of naive T-cells?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Mature dendritic cells are indeed the most potent stimulator of naive T-cells among the options provided. They play a central role in the initiation of adaptive immune responses.
• Dendritic cells are considered professional antigen-presenting cells (APCs). They have several features that make them highly effective at stimulating naive T-cells:
• They have high levels of MHC-II molecules on their surface, which are crucial for presenting antigenic peptides to T-cells.
• They also express costimulatory molecules like CD80 and CD86, which provide necessary signals for T-cell activation.
• Mature dendritic cells are adept at capturing, processing, and presenting antigens to naive T-cells in lymphoid tissues.

Other Options:
• Option B: Follicular dendritic cells (FDCs) have a specialized role within lymphoid tissues, particularly in the germinal centers of secondary lymphoid organs like lymph nodes and spleen. However, it's important to note that FDCs are not involved in directly stimulating naive T-cells.
• The primary function of FDCs is to present antigens to B-lymphocytes. They help B-cells in the process of affinity maturation and class switching during antibody production.
• FDCs are essential for maintaining the structure of germinal centers and supporting B-cell responses.
• Unlike dendritic cells (DCs), which directly engage with T-cells to activate them, FDCs interact mainly with B-cells, aiding in the generation of high-affinity antibodies.
• Option C: B-lymphocytes, or B cells, are a key component of the adaptive immune system responsible for antibody production. While B cells play a crucial role in the immune response, they are not the most potent stimulators of naive T-cells.
• B cells primarily interact with T-helper cells (a subset of T-cells) once they have been activated by antigen presentation from antigen-presenting cells (APCs) like dendritic cells. This interaction is essential for the initiation of antibody production and the development of effective humoral immunity.
• B cells themselves do not directly stimulate naive T-cells as effectively as mature dendritic cells, which are specialized for this purpose.
• Option D: Natural Killer (NK) cells are part of the innate immune system and function to detect and eliminate infected or abnormal cells, particularly those with altered surface markers. They are not specialized for the direct activation of naive T-cells.
• NK cells do not have the same antigen-presenting capabilities as dendritic cells. Their primary role is to provide rapid immune responses against stressed or virally infected cells.

stem: What is the best method to study previous research studies?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• A meta-analysis is a quantitative method for combining and analyzing data from multiple independent studies on a particular research question or topic.
• It entails systematic review of the existing literature, identification of relevant studies, data extraction from each study, and data pooling to obtain an overall summary estimate of the effect or outcome of interest.
• Meta-analysis provides a statistical framework for evaluating the combined evidence from multiple studies, increasing the statistical power and precision of the results.

Other Options:
• Option A: Cohort study: A cohort study is an observational study design in which a group of people (cohort) is followed over time to investigate the occurrence of outcomes or events of interest. Cohort studies can provide useful information about the natural history of diseases, risk factors, and outcomes, but they are not designed to study previous research studies.
• Option B: A systematic review is a comprehensive and structured approach to gathering, evaluating, and synthesizing the available evidence on a specific research question. It entails conducting a systematic search for relevant studies, evaluating their quality, and summarizing the findings. While systematic reviews can provide an in-depth overview of existing research studies, they do not include quantitative data synthesis.
• Option D: RCT (Randomized Controlled Trial): An RCT is a study design used in clinical research to compare the efficacy of various interventions or treatments. It entails randomly assigning participants to different groups (for example, treatment and control groups) and evaluating the outcomes of interest. RCTs generate primary data and are not intended to study previous research studies.

stem: Maximum mortality is associated with which of the following organisms?
subject: Microbiology, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Rabies is a viral disease that affects the central nervous system. Once symptoms appear, it's almost always fatal. However, human-to-human transmission is rare, and most cases result from animal bites.

Other Options:
• Option A: Ebola - Ebola virus causes a severe and often fatal illness known as Ebola virus disease (EVD). While it can lead to high mortality rates during outbreaks, it typically does not spread as easily as some other infectious diseases.
• Option C: Influenza H1N1 - The H1N1 influenza virus caused a global pandemic in 2009. While it spread widely, the overall mortality rate was relatively low compared to the initial concerns.
• Option D: Nipah - Nipah virus is a zoonotic virus that can cause severe respiratory illness in humans. It has caused outbreaks with varying mortality rates, but the overall impact has been limited compared to some other diseases.

subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• The Vaccine Vial Monitor (VVM), is a marker of vaccine heat stability. VVM is a visual indicator on vaccine vials that changes color when exposed to heat, allowing for an assessment of heat stability and potential vaccine efficacy loss.



Other Options:

stem: Discrete probability distribution that expresses probability of a given number of events in a particular time frame is known as?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• The Poisson distribution is a discrete probability distribution that expresses the probability of a given number of events occurring in a given time frame.
• This distribution is commonly used to model the number of events that occur in a given time or space interval. It has a single parameter X that represents the average rate or mean number of events in the given time frame.

Other Options:
• Option A: The binomial distribution is used to calculate the probability of a fixed number of successes in a fixed number of independent trials that are not time-limited.
• Option B: The normal distribution, also known as the Gaussian distribution, is a continuous distribution. It is not restricted to counting events in a specific time frame.
• Option D: The normal distribution is a continuous probability distribution that is not restricted to counting events over time.

stem: You advised a Pap smear test on a trichomoniasis infected 30 year old female. What will you explain, the test is done for?
options: Screening test for cancer of female genital tract
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Screening for cancer cervix
• A Pap smear test, also known as a Pap test or Pap smear, is primarily done to screen for cervical cancer.
• It involves collecting cells from the cervix (the lower part of the uterus) and examining them under a microscope to detect any abnormal changes that could indicate the presence of precancerous or cancerous cells.

Other Options:
• Option B: Diagnostic of cancer cervix
• While a Pap smear is a valuable screening tool, it is not a definitive diagnostic test for cervical cancer.
• If abnormal cells are detected during a Pap smear, further diagnostic tests such as colposcopy and biopsy might be performed to confirm the presence of cancer.
• Option C: Diagnostic test for trichomoniasis
• A Pap smear is not specifically used to diagnose trichomoniasis, which is a sexually transmitted infection caused by a parasite.
• Trichomoniasis is typically diagnosed through specific tests like a vaginal swab or urine test.
• Option D: Screening test for cancer of the female genital tract
• This option is partially correct. A Pap smear is indeed a screening test for various types of cancers within the female genital tract, including cervical cancer.
• It can also help detect certain abnormalities in the vaginal and uterine tissues. However, it's important to note that the Pap smear is specifically designed to screen for cervical cancer, not for all cancers of the female genital tract.

stem: You are a medical officer planning for mass therapy for iron deficiency anemia, which of the following is correct?
subject: PSM, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• For mass prophylaxis or treatment for anemia, a combination of iron supplementation and other essential nutrients that support iron absorption and utilization is frequently recommended.
• Iron deficiency anemia is commonly treated with a combination of FeSO4 (ferrous sulfate) and vitamins C, Folate, and B12.
• Iron (FeSO4) is a necessary mineral for the production of hemoglobin, which transports oxygen in the blood.
• Iron absorption is improved by vitamin C, while folate (Vitamin B9) and vitamin B12 are required for red blood cell production and maturation.

Other Options:
• Option B: Iron (III) hydroxide polymaltose: This is another type of iron supplement, but it is not commonly used for mass prophylaxis or anemia treatment. It may be prescribed in specific cases or for individualized treatment plans, but it is not usually recommended for use on a large scale.
• Option C: Ferrous fumarate: Ferrous fumarate is another type of iron supplement that is commonly used to treat anemia caused by iron deficiency. However, as mentioned in option A, the recommended mass prophylaxis or treatment often includes a combination of iron with other essential nutrients.
• Option D: Inj. iron sucrose: Injectable iron sucrose is a type of iron supplementation that is used when oral iron is not tolerated or is ineffective. Oral iron supplementation, on the other hand, is generally preferred for mass prophylaxis or treatment due to its convenience and cost-effectiveness.

stem: Hand sanitization: Where all it will be used?
options: After touching the patient and surroundings
subject: Microbiology, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• To maintain proper hygiene and minimize the risk of infection transmission, it's best to sanitize your hands before and after touching the patient and their surroundings. This comprehensive approach helps protect both healthcare providers and patients.

Other Options:
• Option A: This is essential to prevent the transfer of germs from your hands to the patient. It helps protect the patient from potential infections.
• Option B: After any physical contact with the patient, it's crucial to sanitize your hands. This prevents the spread of any potential pathogens that the patient might carry.
• Option C: This option is also important. Not only should you sanitize your hands after direct patient contact, but also after touching any surfaces or objects in the patient's environment. These surfaces may harbor harmful germs.

stem: Highest thermic Effect is seen in?
subject: Biochemistry, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Proteins have the highest thermic effect among the options, around 20-30% of the calories consumed.
• This means that a significant portion of the calories you get from protein is used by your body in the process of digestion and metabolism.

Other Options:
• Option A: Carbohydrate diet - Carbohydrates have a thermic effect of around 5-10% of the calories consumed. This means that when you consume carbohydrates, your body expends some energy to digest, absorb, and process them.
• Option C: Fat diet - Fats have a thermic effect of around 0-3% of the calories consumed. They have the lowest thermic effect among the options, meaning your body expends the least amount of energy to digest and process dietary fats.
• Option D: Vitamin diet - Vitamins do not contribute significantly to the thermic effect of food. They play essential roles in various bodily functions, but they are not directly related to the thermic effect of food.

stem: Difference between lowest and highest score in data?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• The range is the data that best explains the difference between the highest and lowest values. The range is a simple measure of dispersion that computes the difference between a dataset's highest and lowest values. It provides a basic understanding of the data's spread or variability.

Other Options:
• Option B: Interquartile range (IQR) is a dispersion measure that represents the middle 50% of the data range, specifically the difference between the third and first quartiles. It is not the correct option because it does not capture the difference between the highest and lowest values.
• Option C: The coefficient of variation is a measure of relative variability that is calculated as the standard deviation divided by the mean. It is not the correct option because it does not directly address the difference between the highest and lowest values.
• Option D: Variance is a dispersion measure that quantifies the average squared deviation from the mean. It is not the correct option because it does not specifically focus on the difference between the highest and lowest values.

stem: A patient with earwax underwent syringing. During syringing, he developed syncope episodes. Stimulation of which of the following nerves is responsible for this?
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
Arnold nerve, the auricular branch of the vagus nerve, is responsible for syncope during ear syringing.
• The external auditory canal receives sensory supply from multiple nerves.
• The auricular branch of the vagus nerve is called Arnold nerve.
• Stimulation of Arnold nerve during ear syringing can trigger a vagal reflex.
• This may cause bradycardia, hypotension, dizziness, cough, or syncope.
• This is also related to Arnold's reflex, where stimulation of the external auditory canal can produce cough.
• Therefore, syncope during ear syringing is due to stimulation of Arnold nerve.

Other Options:
• Option A- Auriculotemporal nerve
• Auriculotemporal nerve is a branch of the mandibular division of trigeminal nerve.
• It supplies part of the external ear and external auditory canal.
• However, vagal symptoms like syncope are classically due to Arnold nerve.
• Hence, this option is incorrect.
• Option B- Chorda tympani
• Chorda tympani is a branch of the facial nerve.
• It carries taste sensation from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue.
• It also carries parasympathetic fibers to submandibular and sublingual glands.
• It is not responsible for syncope during ear syringing.
• Hence, this option is incorrect.
• Option D- Jacobson nerve
• Jacobson nerve is the tympanic branch of the glossopharyngeal nerve.
• It contributes to the tympanic plexus and supplies the middle ear mucosa.
• It is not the nerve classically responsible for vagal syncope during ear syringing.
• Hence, this option is incorrect.

stem: A patient with CSOM presented with Rinne test positive and fistula test positive. Patient refused treatment and came back 2 months later with a negative fistula sign. What is the expected tuning fork test finding in this patient?
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
• CSOM stands for Chronic Suppurative Otitis Media, which is a long-term ear infection that causes inflammation and discharge in the middle ear.
• The Rinne test and fistula test are both tuning fork tests that evaluate the hearing ability of a patient.
• In the Rinne test, a vibrating tuning fork is placed on the mastoid bone behind the ear, and the patient is asked to indicate when the sound is no longer heard. The tuning fork is then moved near the ear canal, and the patient is asked to indicate when the sound is no longer heard. A positive Rinne test indicates that air conduction is greater than bone conduction, which is a normal finding.
• The fistula test evaluates the presence of a defect in the bony labyrinth of the inner ear. The test involves applying pressure to the tragus (a small pointed projection in front of the ear canal) and noting whether the patient experiences vertigo, nystagmus (involuntary eye movements), or both.
• In patients with CSOM, a positive fistula test may indicate a bony erosion in the inner ear due to the chronic inflammation. If the patient refused treatment and returned after 2 months with a negative fistula sign, it may suggest that the bony defect has healed.
• Regarding the expected tuning fork test finding, it is likely that the Rinne test would be true positive, indicating normal air conduction.
• The healing of the bony defect may not have any direct effect on the Rinne test, and the positive finding may persist. Therefore, option C, true positive Rinne test, is the most likely answer.

Other options:
• Option A: False negative Rinne test-This means that the patient would have a conductive hearing loss (such as in CSOM) but the Rinne test would incorrectly indicate a sensorineural hearing loss.
• However, this option is not applicable to the given scenario, as the patient's Rinne test is reported as positive.
• Option B: False positive Rinne test- This means that the patient would have a sensorineural hearing loss but the Rinne test would incorrectly indicate a conductive hearing loss.
• This option is also not applicable to the given scenario, as the patient has a confirmed diagnosis of CSOM, which is a type of conductive hearing loss.
• Option D: True negative Rinne test-This means that the patient has a sensorineural hearing loss and the Rinne test correctly indicates this by showing a negative result (bone conduction is greater than air conduction).
• This option is not applicable to the given scenario as the patient has a confirmed diagnosis of CSOM, which is a type of conductive hearing loss.

stem: A child presents with a history of foreign body ingestion. X ray picture is given below. What is the possible site of foreign body impaction?
subject: ENT, Pediatrics, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• The given X-ray image shows the column of air in front which represents the trachea with white opaque object in the tube behind the air column.
• The tube behind the air column is the esophagus. Hence, the foreign body is lodged in the esophagus.

Other options:
• Option B: When a foreign object becomes lodged in the larynx (also known as the voice box), it can cause symptoms such as choking, coughing, and difficulty breathing.
• If the object completely blocks the airway, it can be life-threatening and require emergency intervention.
• In addition to these symptoms, a physical examination of the larynx may reveal swelling, redness, or irritation.
• Option C: If a foreign object becomes stuck in the trachea (also known as the windpipe), it can cause similar symptoms to those seen with laryngeal foreign body impaction. These may include coughing, choking, and difficulty breathing.
• In some cases, the foreign object may partially obstruct the airway, causing wheezing or other abnormal breath sounds.
• If left untreated, this can also be life-threatening. Examination of the trachea may reveal signs of inflammation or irritation.
• Option D: When a foreign object enters the lungs, it can cause a range of symptoms depending on its size and location.
• Small objects may be coughed up or cleared by the body's immune system, while larger objects may become lodged in the airways and cause symptoms such as coughing, wheezing, and shortness of breath.
• In some cases, a foreign object in the lung may lead to pneumonia or other respiratory infections.
• Examination of the lungs may reveal abnormal breath sounds or signs of inflammation on imaging tests such as chest x-rays or CT scans.

stem: A 55 year-old man undergoes LASIK surgery for the correction of myopia. Which of the following is the best method to check for IOL power calculation?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• The Haigis formula is one of the more advanced methods of intraocular lens (IOL) power calculation and is particularly useful for patients who have had prior refractive surgery, such as LASIK.
• It takes into account factors like the patient's corneal characteristics and is designed to provide more accurate IOL power predictions in such cases.
• It is often considered a reliable choice for post-LASIK IOL power calculations.

Other Options:
• Option A: Hoffer Q is a commonly used formula but may not be the best choice for patients who have had LASIK as it doesn't account for certain post-refractive surgery changes in the cornea.
• Option C: SRK 1 is an older formula that doesn't take into account specific corneal characteristics, which are crucial in post-LASIK cases.
• Option D: SRK 2, while it is an improvement over SRK 1, also lacks some of the refinements and corneal considerations found in more advanced formulas such as the Haigis formula, making it less ideal for post-LASIK IOL calculations.

stem: If the numerical aperture of an objective lens is increased, what is the change in the image observed?
subject: Pathology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Increasing the numerical aperture (NA) of the objective lens can lead to an increase in magnification.
• This is because a higher NA allows the lens to capture more light rays from the specimen, resulting in a larger and more detailed image. The increase in magnification, is however, more significant than the improvement in resolution.

Other Options:
• Option B: Changing the NA primarily affects size and resolution, rather than contrast.
• Option C: Increasing the NA does affect the resolution, however, magnification is more significantly affected than the resolution.
• Option D: Changing the NA does not directly affect the field of view. That is influenced by other factors such as distance.

stem: A 34 year-old male presents to the ophthalmologist with complaints of photophobia and blurry vision for one month. He has a history of ocular surgery. He gives a history of no other abnormalities and is otherwise vitally stable. On examination, the right pupil of the patient is fixed and dilated. The doctor wants to differentiate the fixed dilated pupil. Which of the following drugs can be used in this case?
subject: Ophthalmology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• In a patient with a fixed and dilated pupil, the use of pilocarpine eye drops can help differentiate between various possible causes.
• One of the conditions that can be diagnosed or ruled out is Adie's tonic pupil (tonic pupil syndrome).
• In Adie's tonic pupil, the affected pupil is typically super sensitive to cholinergic agents such as pilocarpine, causing it to constrict more compared to the unaffected pupil.
• This differential response to pilocarpine helps in identifying Adie's tonic pupil.

Other Options:
• Option A: Cocaine is typically used to test for Horner's syndrome. The affected pupil fails to dilate significantly in response to 4% cocaine eye drops due to disruption of sympathetic innervation. This patient does not exhibit signs of Horner's syndrome.
• Option C: Phenylephrine is a sympathomimetic agent that causes pupillary dilation. It is not used to differentiate the cause of a fixed and dilated pupil but may be used to test for other conditions such as anisocoria and to assess pupillary function.
• Option D: Adrenaline or epinephrine is also a sympathomimetic agent that induces pupil dilation. It is not used for differentiation in cases of a fixed dilated pupil.

stem: A 70-year-old man with chronic myopia comes to the physician because of a 4-month history of difficulty seeing distant objects, especially at night, despite wearing glasses. Examination shows bilateral narrowing of peripheral visual fields, cupping of the optic discs, and elevated intraocular pressure. The gonioscopy shows no abnormalities. He has been prescribed a drug. Which of the following is a true statement regarding primary open-angle glaucoma?
options: It is associated with diabetes mellitus.
Can occur due to prolonged steroid use
Family history is not a risk factor
Myopics have a higher risk of developing primary open angle glaucoma
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• This patient has a gradual loss of peripheral vision, cupping of the optic discs and elevated intraocular pressure (IOP)- these are hallmark signs of primary open angle glaucoma (POAG).
• Absence of abnormalities in gonioscopy suggests that the angle between the iris and the cornea is open, characteristic of POAG.
• Myopia (nearsightedness) is considered a risk factor for primary open angle glaucoma.
• Individuals with myopia have a higher susceptibility to developing this type of glaucoma, which can lead to elevated intraocular pressure and optic nerve damage.
• Regular eye examinations are essential for myopic individuals to monitor for the development of glaucoma.

Other Options:
• Option A: POAG is not primarily associated with diabetes mellitus. While diabetes can increase the risk of certain eye conditions, it is not a primary cause of POAG.
• Option B: Prolonged steroid use leads to a form of glaucoma known as steroid-induced glaucoma, and not POAG.
• Option C: Family history is a well-established risk factor for POAG. Having a family history of the condition increases an individual's risk of developing it themselves.

stem: A 45-year-old male presents to the eye OPD where you are working as a resident with the following complaint of right eye pain with redness associated with photophobia and reduced vision in the right eye for 3 days. On examination, there are dark, floating spots in the field of vision. Four days back, he had a history of right eye cataract surgery. On examination, his left eye was unremarkable. His right eye visual acuity is 12/20 with a pinhole. He had circumcorneal congestion and grade 3+ cells in the anterior chamber. No significant history of systemic disease. Which of the following option best describes the patient's condition?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• Acute anterior uveitis or iritis is characterised by inflammation primarily in the anterior segment of the eye, which includes the iris and ciliary body.
• This condition often presents with symptoms of eye pain, redness, photophobia, reduced vision and floaters.
• Examination findings of circumcorneal congestion and cells in the anterior chamber are consistent with acute anterior uveitis.

Other Options:
• Option B: This primarily involves inflammation in the posterior segment of the eye, including the retina and choroid. This patient's symptoms and signs are more consistent with anterior segment involvement.
• Option C: Iridocyclitis is another term for anterior uveitis. However, it doesn't specify whether it is acute or chronic, making this an incomplete answer.
• Option D: Chronic iridocyclitis refers to long term inflammation in the anterior segment of the eye, typically lasting weeks to months. The patient's symptoms of sudden onset and the acute nature are not in line with chronic iridocyclitis.

stem: Primary open-angle glaucoma (POAG) is a subset defined by an open, normal appearing anterior chamber angle and raised intraocular pressure (IOP) with no other underlying disease. Which of the following drugs is not given in primary open-angle glaucoma?
subject: Ophthalmology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Primary open-angle glaucoma (POAG) is a chronic eye condition characterised by elevated intraocular pressure (IOP) and damage to the optic nerve. The mainstays for treatment involve lowering the IOP to prevent further optic nerve damage. This includes mitotic agents like pilocarpine, PG analogues, beta-blockers and alpha agonists.
• Amoxicillin is an antibiotic medication and is primarily used in the treatment of bacterial infections, such as URTIs and LRTIs, UTIs and skin infections.
• It is not used as a treatment option for POAG as this glaucoma is not caused by bacteria or infection.

Other Options:
• Option B: Pilocarpine is a mitotic agent that constricts the pupil and can help lower the IOP by increasing the outflow of aqueous humour.
• Option C: Physostigmine is a cholinergic agent that can also lower IOP by increasing aqueous outflow.
• Option D: Phenylephrine is a sympathomimetic agent that dilates the pupil and is sometimes used in combination with other medications to manage IOP in glaucoma.

stem: What is the recommended dose of folic acid in a female with a history of NTD in the last pregnancy?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• When there is a history of a neural tube abnormality in a prior delivery, prenatal folic acid therapy at a dose of 4 mg daily is prescribed.
• Starting one month before conception, therapy is continued throughout the first trimester.
• 400 micrograms of folic acid per day should be provided to all females who are of reproductive age.
• In cases where there is a significant demand, an additional dose (4 mg) should be given. These situations include multiple pregnancies, anticonvulsant medication, hemoglobinopathies, and any related chronic illness or infection.

• In post-term pregnancy, it reduces to 200 ml at 43 weeks.

• 4th visit: Upto 30 weeks
• 5th visit: Upto 34 weeks
• 6th visit: Upto 36 weeks
• 7th visit: Upto 38 weeks
• 8th visit: Upto 40 weeks
• (b) Minimum: 4
• 1st - In first trimester (from 8 to 12 week)
• 2nd - From 14 to 26 weeks
• 3rd - From 28 to 34 weeks
• 4th - 36 weeks till delivery

stem: Cardiac output comes to normal after how many days after delivery?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• After the fifth week of pregnancy, the cardiac output (CO) begins to rise, peaking at 40-50% at around 30-34 weeks.
• After that, the CO remains stationary until term, at which point an observation is made while lying lateral to the subject.
• CO is lowest when seated or lying flat, and it is highest when kneeling or bending at the chest or in the right or left lateral posture.
• Cardiac output rises even more compared to pre-labor readings during labor (+50%) and immediately after birth (+70%).
• Around the second week, blood volume reaches non-pregnant levels.
• CO returns to pre-labor levels within an hour of birth, and to the non-pregnant level within a week (close to 10 days).

subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution

SymptomsNO pruritus initially, then out of proportion pruritus. Plaque on vaginal wall on removal causes Petechiae.Severe irritation & severe pruritus. COLPITIS MACULARIS [STRAWBERRY VAGINA]Fishy odorMainly responsible for infertility. Causes urethritis, PID and tubal damage.
