stem: A lady with infertility undergoes a laparoscopy for the purpose of assessment of her pelvic anatomy. For tubal patency, a chromotubation involves pushing dye from a cannula in the cervix which is observed to come out from the fallopian tubes. This chromotubation is usually done using which of the following?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Diagnostic laparoscopy - If hysterosalpingography reveals abnormal or unclear findings, laparoscopy is advised. It may show the existence of endometriosis, block tubes, unclear findings, or salpingography.
• Diagnostic laparoscopy for infertility assessment includes chromopertubation using methylene blue dye to assess tubal patency.

Other Options:
• Myomectomy, hysterectomy, and endometriosis ablation procedures, all involve the use of a Veress needle and carbon dioxide to create pneumoperitoneum.
• By seeing the uniform distension of the abdomen and doing the Palmer test, which involves injecting 5 ml of saline with a Veress needle, it is possible to determine whether the pneumoperitoneum is properly formed.
• The absence of saline aspiration suggests that the needle was inserted correctly.

stem: Colposcopy filter color is?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Filters in colposcopy improve features for better viewing of the vagina and cervix. Blood artery branching patterns and angle analysis are done using a green filter.
• Colposcopy Examination - After applying 3-5% acetic acid, the cervix and upper vagina are examined under magnification with white light and a green filter. Colposcopy reveals aberrant areas as acetowhite patches, mosaics, punctuation, and abnormal arteries.
• The following are the purposes of colposcopy:
• To investigate the cervix after a Pap smear revealed abnormal cells.
• To find abnormal areas and obtain a biopsy.
• To determine the size of an abnormal lesion.
• To do conservative surgery while under colposcopic supervision.
• To monitor patients who received conservative therapy.

stem: Mutation in which gene is seen in Wilson disease?
subject: Medicine, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Wilson's disease:
• 1. Autosomal recessive inheritance.
• 2. Gene defect - ATP7B Chromosome 13.
• 3. Impaired Cupric ion secretion into the bile which is associated with Cu2+ accumulation in the liver and liver damage.

• Clinical features:
• 1. Cirrhosis - chronic liver disease.
• 2. Sunflower cataract.
• 3. Kayser-Fleischer ring in cornea.
• 4. Arthralgia.
• 5. Cupric ion mediated hemolysis.
• 6. Neurological symptoms.

• Diagnosis:
• 1. Increased urine Cu2+ concentration.
• 2. Decreased serum ceruloplasmin.
• 3. Liver biopsy - Mallory hyaline bodies.
• 4. Macro vesicular steatosis.

• Treatment:
• 1. Reduce dietary Cu2+.
• 2. Chelating agents for Cu2+.
• 3. D-penicillamine.
• 4. Vit B6 supplement.

Other Options:
• Option A: Gene defect-ATP7A-Menkes disease. Associated with - 1. Impaired copper metabolism. 2. Neurological manifestations.
• Option C: Gene defect ATP7C - also causes Menkes disease.
• Option D: Gene defect ATP7D is not associated with Wilson's disease.

stem: The condition seen in this child could be due to which of the following?
options: Kwashiorkor due to less protein and calorie intake
Kwashiorkor due to less calorie intake
Marasmus due to low protein and calorie intake
subject: Pediatrics, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• General appearance of the child in the given image looks like a healthy "fat sugar baby appearance" because of edema.
• Kwashiorkor -
• 1. Occurs due to protein energy malnutrition.
• 2. It usually affects 1-4 years old children.
• Clinical features -
• 1. General appearance - Fat sugar baby appearance.
• 2. Edema pitting edema is the main sign of kwashiorkor.
• 3. Muscle wasting - always present. The child is hypotonic, weak and unable to walk or stand.
• 4. Hair - dyspigmentation of hair and sparseness over occipital and temporal region.
• 5. Skin changes - Area of dyspigmentation and hyperpigmentation. Cracks on the skin surface.
• 6. Neurological changes - tremors are seen during recovery.
• 7. Mental changes - It includes unhappiness, irritability and intermittent cry.
• 8. Anemia - nutritional anemia.

Other Options:
• Option B: Kwashiorkor due to less calorie intake - kwashiorkor is associated with both protein and calories malnutrition. Protein deficiency is prominent in kwashiorkor which causes edema.
• Option C & D: Marasmus - in marasmus child appears very thin due to severe wasting. Marasmus: It is a form of protein energy malnutrition. It is a severe form of wasting. Precipitating factors acute starvation or acute illness. Clinical features: Severe wasting main sign of marasmus. The child has no fat and appears very thin. 1. Monkey facies due to loss of buccal pad or fat. 2. Baggy pants appearance loose skin of buttocks. 3. Edema - no edema. 4. Affects children may appear alert.

stem: A 4-year old child presented to emergency following a sudden fall at school. His pH was 7.2, pCO2 was 64, HCO3 was 26, pO2 was 44. The most likely diagnosis is?
subject: Pediatrics, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Blood gas analysis shows - High pCO2-64 and low pO2-44 (Hypoxemia).
• Low blood pH and normal level of HCO3 are suggestive of compensated acidosis.
• The given condition is suggestive of respiratory failure.
• Normal blood pH - 7.35-7.45.
• Normal blood pO2 - 75-100 mmHg.
• Normal blood pCO2 - 35-45 mmHg.
• Normal blood bicarbonate - 22-29 mEq/L.
• Respiratory Failure - It is a condition in which the respiratory system fails to maintain adequate gas exchange, resulting in low levels of pO2 and/or high levels of pCO2 in the blood.
• pO2 < 60 mmHg and pCO2 more than 50 mmHg is suggestive of respiratory failure.
• Causes of Respiratory Failure - 1. Acute respiratory distress Syndrome. 2. Lung diseases-asthma, COPD, pulmonary fibrosis. 3. Injury and trauma. 4. Cardiac failure. 5. Drug overdose - sedatives and opioids.

Other Options:
• Option A: Pneumonia - It is infection of the lungs. It can also result in hypoxemia and respiratory failure however in the given case blood gas analysis shows respiratory acidosis, which is not typical pneumonia.
• Option B: Salicylate poisoning - Salicylate poisoning causes respiratory alkalosis rather than respiratory acidosis.
• Option C: Acute renal failure - Acute renal failure is a condition in which the kidneys suddenly stop working properly. It can cause metabolic acidosis, which would be reflected in the ABG values. However, in this case, the bicarbonate level is normal, suggesting that the kidneys are compensating for respiratory acidosis rather than causing metabolic acidosis.

stem: Which drug is preferred in premature neonates for closure of PDA?
subject: Pediatrics, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Ibuprofen is a superior drug in premature neonates for closure of PDA than other NSAIDS due low range of side effects and higher efficiency.
• Oral Ibuprofen used in premature neonates for closure of PDA.
• Mechanism of action - NSAIDS inhibits PGs synthesis, which maintains patency of ductus arteriosus.
• PDA (Patent Ductus Arteriosus) - Ductus Arteriosus is a blood vessel that connects the pulmonary artery to the aorta.
• Usually it closes after birth. However, in some cases it fails to close resulting in PDA.
• Associated infection- Rubella.
• Clinical features - 1. Left to right shunting. 2. Differential cyanosis (lower limb cyanosis and upper limb pink). 3. Loud continuous murmur.
• Medical Closure of PDA - PGs inhibitors are used in preterm babies-e.g. NSAIDS like ibuprofen and indomethacin.
• Ibuprofen is preferred over indomethacin due to less side effects.

Other Options:
• Option B: Indomethacin - IV indomethacin also used in closure of PDA. Ibuprofen is preferred over indomethacin due to less side effects.
• Option C: Mefenamic acid - It is sometimes used as an alternative to ibuprofen and indomethacin for closure of PDA. However, there is limited evidence for safety and efficacy of mefenamic acid for PDA closure.
• Option D: Paracetamol - Also known as acetaminophen. Paracetamol commonly used as a pain reliever and fever reducer. Paracetamol is usually not used for PDA.

stem: Sequestrum is a piece of dead bone that has become separated during the process of necrosis from the normal or sound bone. Ring sequestrum is seen in which of the following conditions?
options: Infection at pinhole of external fixator
subject: orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• Ring sequestrum is seen in infection at the pinhole of the external fixator.
• This is due to Excessive periosteal stripping near the end of the bone.
• Sequestrum is a fragment of dead bone that is being "eaten" away by diseased granulation tissue. It appears pale and has a rough outer surface because the surrounding granulation tissue is continually eroding the smooth inner surface.
• An involucrum is the thick, sclerotic bone that sits on top of a sequestrum.
• The involucrum may have a few openings where pus can escape. They are known as cloacae.

Other Options:
• Option A: Salmonella infection - This occurs after a typhoid fever attack during the convalescent stage. This causes Osteomyelitis of the subacute variety that typically affects the ulna, tibia, or vertebra. Bones are damaged in a bilaterally symmetrical pattern. Among radiological findings, diaphyseal sclerosis is the most prominent one. The condition affects people with sickle cell anaemia more frequently.
• Option B: Tuberculosis causes subacute osteomyelitis of the shaft. These lesions are frequently multifocal. A key radiographic aspect is that, unlike in pyogenic osteomyelitis, the bone lysis is out of proportion to the new bone growth. In some instances, a biopsy may be required. of shafts of long bones.
• Option C: After radiation, two things can occur in a patient - osteosarcoma and radiation osteitis (osteoradionecrosis).

stem: A 40-year-old female presents to the clinic complaining of bilateral wrist pain. She also reports fatigue and low-grade fever. On further questioning, she states that she has morning stiffness in her hands that lasts more than one hour. On examination, swelling of the bilateral wrist is noted. IgM antibody against the Fc component of the IgG is detected in the serum. X-ray shows destructive arthritis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: orthopaedics, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Above clinical scenario showing women having bilateral wrist pain, low grade fever, morning stiffness in hands and X-ray showing destructive arthritis is suggestive of diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis.
• The most typical cause of persistent inflammatory joint illness is rheumatoid arthritis (RA).
• The most common symptoms include morning stiffness, symmetrical polyarthritis and tenosynovitis, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and the presence of autoantibodies, such as rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-citrullinated peptide antibodies (ACPAs), in the serum.
• This involves small joints. The wrists, foot, knees, and shoulders are the joints where the symptoms "spread" most frequently as time goes on.
• Generalised stiffness following periods of inactivity, particularly after getting out of bed in the morning, is another defining characteristic. Usually, this morning stiffness lasts for more than 30 minutes.

Other Options:
• Option A: Ankylosing spondylitis can be ruled out because in this case, spine and sacroiliac joints are most commonly involved. This is characterised by back pain and stiffness, with varying degrees of hip and shoulder involvement and (less frequently) peripheral joint involvement. The genetic markers HLA-B27 and ERAP1 strongly favour familial association.
• Option C: Reiter's syndrome can be ruled out because in this, there is a triad of urethritis, arthritis and conjunctivitis. This typically occurs a few weeks after either dysentery or a genitourinary infection. It is an aseptic inflammatory arthritis linked to an unspecified infection. It is likely the most prevalent form of large-joint polyarthritis. Shigella dysentery or non-specific urogenital infection are thought to cause it in 1-3% of patients overall. Men are more frequently impacted than women.
• Option D: Lupus arthritis can be ruled out because other clinical features of lupus are not present in this patient. Although one of the most typical illness symptoms in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is arthritis. SLE can affect almost every joint, however the most common ones to be affected are the hands and knees.

stem:
Osteoporosis is defined as a decrease in bone mineral density with resultant trabecular and cortical bone loss. Patients with osteoporosis usually have normal serum calcium, phosphate, alkaline phosphatase, and parathyroid hormone level. Which of the following tests is commonly used for osteoporosis?
1. DEXA
2. Bone scan
3. Quantitative CT
4. Chemical analysis
5. X-RAY
subject: orthopaedics, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• DEXA, Bone scan, Chemical analysis and X-Ray are commonly used for osteoporosis.
• You can evaluate your bone mineral density and bone loss with a DEXA scan, a high-precision type of X-ray.
• There is a higher risk of osteoporosis and bone fractures if your bone density is lower than is typical for your age. It is gold standard for determining bone mass is the DEXA scan.
• Bone scintigraphy, often known as a bone scan is a nuclear medicine imaging procedure for the bone. Numerous bone disorders, such as cancer of the bone or metastases, the location of bone inflammation, and fractures (which may not be seen in conventional X-ray pictures), can be diagnosed with this technique.
• Chemical analysis in osteoporosis shows- Alkaline phosphatase, phosphates, and serum calcium are all within normal ranges. Plasma albumin and total plasma proteins are low.
• Quantitative CT is not a commonly used test for osteoporosis.

Other Options:
• Option A: This option includes DEXA Scan, Bone scan and quantitative CT. Although DEXA scan and bone scan are commonly used for osteoporosis but quantitative CT is not a commonly used technique, this is a comparatively advance technique. Moreover, X ray and chemical analysis are not included in this option. Thats why this option is incorrect.
• Option B: This option includes Bone scan and quantitative CT. Quantitative CT is not a commonly used technique for osteoporosis. Additionally, other commonly used techniques DEXA, X-Ray and chemical analysis are not included in this option. Thats why thos option is incorrect.
• Option C: This option includes Quantitative CT, chemical analysis and X-Ray. Quantitative CT is an advanced technique to measure bone density and this is currently not commonly used for osteoporosis. Also, two commonly used technique DEXA scan and bone scan are not included in this option. Thats why this option is incorrect.

stem: Which of the following brace is shown in the image?
subject: orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• Above brace shown in the image is the Milwaukee brace used in patients having scoliosis.
• The Milwaukee brace is designed to fit tightly over the pelvis, below Chin and head pads encourage active postural correction, and a thoracic pad presses against the ribs at the curve's peak.
• For many years, bracing has been utilised to treat increasing scoliotic curves between 20 and 30 degrees.
• Success is based on compliance, thus they must be worn for more than 20 hours per day, only coming off for exercise and washing.

Other Options:
• Option A: The Boston brace, a form of back brace called a thoraco-lumbo-sacral-orthosis (TLSO) is predominantly used to treat childhood idiopathic scoliosis in these patients.
• Option C: Taylor Brace (Short/Long Type) is a lightweight spinal brace that covers all of the dorsal, lumbar, and sacral vertebrae, supports and immobilises the spine in neutral position, and still allows for the necessary range of motion in the body.
• Option D: The Ash Brace is made to hold the spinal column immobile and to keep it in a neutral or slightly hyperextended condition. It supports cervical and lumbar lordosis and stabilises the spine, alleviating pressure on the anterior portions of the vertebral bodies.

stem: 10 year old male child with pain around the right knee joint on X-ray there is a lesion at distal femur extending up to terminal end, following are the X-ray image of the patient. What is the diagnosis?
subject: orthopaedics, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• Above clinical scenario and X ray showing a lesion at distal end extending to terminal end is suggestive of diagnosis of osteosarcoma.
• The bimodal age distribution of osteosarcoma, the most prevalent primary malignant bone cancer, peaks in adolescence (10-14 years) then again in the seventh decade.
• The majority are found at the distal femur, proximal tibia, proximal femur, and humerus, and are typically metaphyseal or metadiaphyseal.
• Patients express increasing pain and swelling, especially if they have nighttime pain, non-mechanical pain, or joint restriction.
• X-rays are typically used for diagnosis and typically reveal an ill-defined, permeative bone-forming lesion that is causing cortical damage and periosteal reaction like sunburst appearance and codman triangle are seen.
• A wide resection and limb reconstruction are carried out following re-staging to assess the effectiveness of induction chemotherapy.

Other Options:
• Option A: Giant cell tumor can be ruled out because this is a benign tumor and mostly present between age 20 and 45 years and also this periosteal reaction is not seen in giant cell tumor. It is a benign yet aggressive bone tumor made up of a proliferation of mononuclear cells and sporadic macrophages. Patients usually complain of pain and, less frequently, an expanding mass, especially near the knee. X ray shows lytic lesions with soap bubble appearance.
• Option C: Osteoid osteoma can be ruled out because this is also a benign tumor which is made of braided and osteoid bone that is encircled by reactive bone. These lesions are more frequent in young individuals, but they are uncommon in children under 5 and similarly uncommon in people over 30. Men experience them more frequently than women. In the long bones, notably the proximal femur, it is most frequently observed. The most common symptom of osteoid osteomas is pain, which is typically worse at night and is usually treated with non-steroidal anti-inflammatories.
• Option D: Chondroblastoma can be ruled out because it is a benign tumor that often appears like a well circumscribed round or oval lytic lesions in the center of epiphysis of a long bone. This is mostly present in the 10-20 age group. It presents as a painful mass that causes effusion or stiffness.

stem: A 15-year-old boy presented to the emergency department with difficulty breathing after being stung by a bee. On examination, lung wheezes are heard, and his lips are swollen. Blood pressure is 80/55 mm hg. Epinephrine is injected as part of the management protocol. What is the correct route and concentration of adrenaline given to such a patient?
subject: Medicine, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Above clinical scenario showing difficulty in breathing (due to bronchoconstriction) after stung by bee, along with swollen lips, hypotension and wheezes in lungs is suggestive of anaphylactic reaction.
• 0.3-0.5 mL of 1:1000 (1 mg/mL) epinephrine intramuscular is used for management of this condition.
• It can also be given subcutaneously but intramuscular is preferred.
• Anaphylaxis happens when an antigen interacts with the immunoglobulin E, causing basophils and mast cells to release inflammatory substances.
• It is essential to identify an anaphylactic reaction as soon as possible because it can be fatal in minutes to hours after the onset of symptoms.

Other Options:
• Option B: IV 1:10000 epinephrine can be used in case of refractory anaphylactic shock and cardiac arrest patients.
• Option C: 1:100000 epinephrine is used as an additive to local anesthetics. This addition is done to decrease absorption of local anesthetics.
• Option D: 1: 200000 epinephrine is also used as an additive to local anesthetics. This addition is done to decrease absorption of local anesthetics.

stem: BLUE pipeline in OT for which anesthetic gas?
subject: Anaesthesia
explanation:
Solution
• Blue pipeline in OT is which for nitrous oxide gas.
• Anaesthesia machines are used to administer the medicinal gas nitrous oxide.
• It is used with oxygen and a number of anaesthetics.
• Thus, nitrous oxide is only present in operating rooms.

Other Options:
• Option A: Oxygen is employed as a medicinal gas to maintain life. In addition, it is utilised to power ventilators and anaesthesia equipment in addition to conventional manual ventilation techniques. White is the colour code for oxygen.
• Option C: Medical air is used as a carrier gas for anaesthetic medicines, as a power source for surgical tools in the operating theatre, and to decrease exposure to high concentrations of oxygen. Black is the designated colour.
• Option D: A vacuum central plant is used in combination with the vacuum system. Actually, it is a negative pressure that is used to suction patients and scavenge anaesthetic gas, not a gas. Vacuum is often given at a pressure that is 400 mmHg (53 KPa) lower than atmospheric pressure. Yellow pipes are recognised by their colour.

stem: All of the following are seen in MEN 2B syndrome except?
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Parathyroid adenoma is not seen in multiple endocrine neoplasia 2B (MEN 2B) syndrome.
• Multiple endocrine neoplasia (MEN) describes conditions with tumors involving two or more endocrine glands. There are four types MEN 1-4. They have an autosomal dominant pattern of inheritance and can also be sporadic.
• MEN type 2 comprises groups of disorders caused by gain-of-function mutations of the RET proto-oncogene at chromosomal locus 10q11.2.
• There are three variants: MEN 2A, also known as MEN 2 (most common type). MEN 2B, also known as MEN 3. MTC only.
• MEN 2A is also known as Sipple's syndrome. It is characterized by medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC), pheochromocytomas, and parathyroid tumors.
• In MEN 2B along with MTC and pheochromocytoma, it is associated with other abnormalities such as mucosal neuromas, marfanoid habitus, medullated corneal nerve fibers, and megacolon.
• Parathyroid tumors are generally not seen here, in contrast to the MEN 2A type.
• MTC presents early and is usually aggressive. MTC is the most common feature, presents as a palpable mass in the neck, and can be associated with pressure symptoms.
• Ectopic adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) secreted by the tumor can cause Cushing's syndrome.
• Pheochromocytoma is frequently bilateral.
• Palpitations, sweating, and headaches are the features of pheochromocytoma.
• Mucosal neuromas can present with bumpy and enlarged lips, tongue, and eyelids.
• Intestinal autonomic ganglion dysfunction can lead to megacolon and diverticula.
• Medullated corneal fibers and delayed puberty can also be seen.
• Marfanoid habitus marked by tall stature, long limbs, arachnodactyly, and joint hypermobility is an associated feature.
• Other options are incorrect.

stem: A male patient presented with a 0.3 cm nodule on the left nasolabial fold. It was excised and a pathological examination was done. The image is given below. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Pathology, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• Basal cell carcinoma is most likely the cause of the nasolabial fold nodule and peripheral palisading seen on the histological imaging described in the clinical situation.
• The slow-growing malignant tumour known as basal cell carcinoma has hallmarks of local invasion. The pilosebaceous skin is affected.
• The main risk factor is exposure to ultraviolet light, exposure to ionising radiation, coal, tar, aromatic compounds, and arsenic.
• It primarily affects white people and older or middle-aged men who have overexposed skin that are frequent in the age range of 40 to 80.
• It is categorised as localised and generalised on a macroscopic level.
• Ovoid cells in nests with a single palisading layer are visible by histopathological characteristics.
• Incomplete excision can result in aggression since the outer layer of cells is actively dividing.
• The management employs Moh's microsurgery. Non-surgical options like imiquimod and topical 5-fluorouracil are helpful.
• Metastasis is incredibly rare.

Other options:
• Option B: Melanoma is a malignancy commonly seen in young adults arising from melanocytes and involves the skin, mucosa, retina, and leptomeninges. Microscopically, malignant changes are seen in the basal epidermis with breach and invasion of the dermis.
• Option C: Squamous cell carcinoma is the second most common malignant tumor and it is seen more commonly in men. Microscopically, the proliferation of irregular masses of squamous epithelium is seen.
• Option D: Nevus are aggregates of melanocytes at the dermis or dermo-epidermal junction. It can get transformed into malignant melanoma.

stem: A patient presented with one and a half months of amenorrhea, associated with abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding with normal blood pressure. Investigations revealed beta-hCG to be 1400mIU/mL. An ultrasound scan was done which showed a trilaminar endometrium with normal adnexa. What is the next best step for managing this patient?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• This case raises the possibility of an acute ectopic pregnancy.
• It is associated with cases of extensive intraperitoneal bleeding during tubal abortion or tubal rupture.
• b-HCG test is the most sensitive method for identifying an ectopic pregnancy.
• The classical triad of acute ectopic pregnancy symptoms is as follows:
• 1. Amenorrhea - Short period of 6-8 weeks (usually)
• 2. Abdominal pain
• 3. Vaginal bleeding
• Investigation for the diagnosis of acute ectopic pregnancy:
• Estimation of b-hCG: Pregnancy test for urine-ELISA is accurate in 95% of ectopic pregnancies and sensitive to 10-50 mlU/mL
• Doubling in 48 hours - Normal intrauterine pregnancy
• Doubling in 5-7 days - Ectopic pregnancy
• Combination of quantitative b-hCG values and sonography: A well-formed intrauterine gestational sac can be seen using TVS as early as 4-5 weeks after the last menstrual period.
• Discriminatory zone-b-HCG level beyond which we must see a sac-
• TVS - >1500 IU
• TAS - >6500 IU

Other options:
• Option B: Repeat ultrasound after 3 days - Transvaginal sonography (TVS) is more informative for diagnosis of early normal/ectopic pregnancy. 4 weeks gestational sac can be seen by TVS.
• Option C: Measurement of progesterone - < 5 ng/mL - Indicates an ectopic or aberrant intrauterine pregnancy. > 25 ng/mL - Indicates a healthy intrauterine pregnancy.
• Option D: Laparoscopy offers benefit in cases of confusion with other pelvic lesions.

stem: A 42-year-old female presented with pain in the right upper quadrant on the fifth postoperative day following laparoscopic cholecystectomy. She is icteric. On ultrasound, there is fluid collection seen in Morrison's pouch. A biliary leak is suspected. Which is the most sensitive investigation to detect this pathology?
subject: Surgery, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• In this clinical scenario, the patient has developed symptoms and signs suggestive of a biliary leak after a laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Morrison's pouch fluid collection seen on ultrasound raises suspicion for this condition. To confirm the presence of a biliary leak and to determine its location, a HIDA scan is the most sensitive diagnostic test.
• A HIDA scan involves injecting a radioactive tracer that is taken up by the liver and excreted into the biliary system. If there is a leak, the tracer will be seen outside the normal biliary system, indicating the site of leakage. This test is highly sensitive for detecting biliary leaks and is a valuable diagnostic tool in such cases.

Other Options:
• Option A: ERCP (Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangiopancreatography) - While ERCP can be used to diagnose and treat biliary tract disorders, it is typically reserved for therapeutic interventions and may not be as sensitive as HIDA for detecting leaks.
• Option B: MRCP (Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography) - MRCP is a non-invasive imaging technique that provides detailed images of the biliary system. While it can be helpful, it may not be as sensitive as HIDA for detecting small biliary leaks.
• Option D: CECT (Contrast-enhanced Computed Tomography) - CECT can detect fluid collections and abnormalities in the abdomen, but it may not be as specific for diagnosing biliary leaks compared to HIDA.

stem: A 55-year-old patient with a history of peptic ulcer disease has had abdominal pain and distension for many days. It has worsened in the last two hours. His blood pressure is 90/40 mmHg. Physical examination shows abdominal guarding. Abdominal radiography is shown. What is the most important next step in the management of this patient?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• In this case, the patient presents with a history of peptic ulcer disease, worsening abdominal pain, distension, hypotension (blood pressure of 90/40 mmHg), and abdominal guarding. The combination of these symptoms and findings raises concerns about a potential perforated peptic ulcer or another serious abdominal pathology.
• The correct approach in such a critical condition is to administer intravenous (IV) fluids to address hypotension and stabilize the patient. However, an exploratory laparotomy is the most important next step in the management. This surgical procedure involves opening the abdomen to directly visualize and assess the source of the problem. If there is a perforated peptic ulcer or another surgical emergency, it can be addressed during the laparotomy.

Other Options:
• Option B: Observation - In a patient with worsening abdominal pain, hypotension, and guarding, observation without immediate intervention is not appropriate. Rapid assessment and intervention are necessary.
• Option C: USG abdomen (Ultrasound of the abdomen) - While ultrasound can be a useful diagnostic tool, it is not the most important immediate step in a patient with this clinical presentation and instability.
• Option D: Antibiotics - While antibiotics may be used as part of the treatment for certain abdominal conditions, they should not be the first and most important step in a patient with signs of a surgical emergency like a perforated ulcer.

stem: A 1-year-old child presented with anemia, hepatosplenomegaly, cervical lymph nodes and multiple scalp swellings with the given skull radiograph. He is diagnosed with multiple myeloma and has multiple lytic lesions on the skull X-ray. Which of the following drug is commonly used in this scenario?
subject: Pediatrics, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Vinblastine is commonly used in the treatment of certain types of leukemia (cancer of the white blood cells), including acute myeloid leukemia (AML, ANLL) and acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL), as well as Hodgkin's lymphoma (Hodgkin's disease) and non-Hodgkin's lymphoma.
• In the context of pediatric multiple myeloma with lytic lesions on the skull X-ray, it can be part of the treatment regimen. Vinblastine is a chemotherapy drug that can help target and treat the cancerous cells in these conditions.

Other Options:
• Option B: Vincristine is another chemotherapy drug used in the treatment of various childhood cancers, including certain types of leukemia and lymphoma. While it is an important chemotherapy agent, it is not typically the first-line treatment for multiple myeloma, especially in children.
• Option C: Methotrexate is primarily used to treat inflammatory conditions such as arthritis and psoriasis. It is not typically the primary drug of choice for multiple myeloma, especially in pediatric cases.
• Option D: Cyclosporine is an immunosuppressive agent used to treat organ rejection post-transplant. It is not a standard treatment for multiple myeloma, regardless of the patient's age.

stem: A 40-year-old male patient with a 2-year history of mucopurulent cough underwent a CT-chest. The CT scan is shown below. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• The most likely diagnosis for the 40-year-old male patient with a 2-year history of mucopurulent cough based on the CT scan shown is Bronchiectasis (Option A).
• Bronchiectasis is a condition characterized by the abnormal widening and thickening of the bronchial tubes (airways) in the lungs. This can lead to a persistent, productive cough with mucus.
• It often results from chronic inflammation.
• Classic clinical signs of bronchiectasis include a persistent cough with daily production of mucopurulent sputum, which can last for months to years.
• Hemoptysis (blood-streaked sputum) may occur due to airway damage during acute infections.
• Other non-specific symptoms can include dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, wheezing, fever, weakness, and weight loss.
• Imaging, such as CT scans, can reveal dilated and thickened bronchi, which are characteristic findings in bronchiectasis.
• Key features in diagnosing bronchiectasis through CT include:
• Bronchus visualized within 1 cm of the pleural surface, especially near costal pleura, which is a helpful sign for early cylindrical changes.
• Lack of tapering in the bronchi.
• An increased Broncho-arterial ratio, with the bronchus measuring approximately 0.65-1.0 times that of the adjacent pulmonary artery branch.
• A ratio greater than 1.5 may indicate bronchiectasis, especially when it's significantly above the normal range, which can be seen in individuals living at high altitudes.
• Based on macroscopic morphology, three types of bronchiectasis have been described, representing a spectrum of severity:
• 1. Cylindrical bronchiectasis: Bronchi have a uniform caliber, do not taper, and have parallel walls, often referred to as the "tram track sign" and "signet ring sign." This is the most common form.
• 2. Varicose bronchiectasis: Less common, characterized by beaded appearances where dilated bronchi have interspersed sites of relative narrowing.
• 3. Cystic bronchiectasis: A severe form with cyst-like bronchi that extend to the pleural surface. Air-fluid levels are commonly present in this type.

Other Options:
• Option B: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a genetic disorder that primarily affects the respiratory and digestive systems. It results from mutations in the CFTR gene, leading to the production of thick and sticky mucus in various organs, including the lungs. CF often presents early in life, typically in childhood, and it can lead to chronic respiratory issues. Patients with CF are prone to recurrent lung infections, which can result in bronchiectasis over time. However, CF-related bronchiectasis typically starts at a younger age than the patient described in the question (a 40-year-old). Key clinical features of CF include chronic cough, frequent lung infections, difficulty breathing, and poor growth in children due to malabsorption of nutrients.
• Option C: Sarcoidosis is a multisystem inflammatory disease characterized by the formation of granulomas (small clusters of immune cells) in various organs, including the lungs. It can present with a wide range of symptoms and can affect people of all ages. While sarcoidosis can involve the lungs and cause respiratory symptoms, the classic presentation does not typically include a persistent mucopurulent cough as described in the question. Instead, patients may experience cough, shortness of breath, and chest pain, but these symptoms are usually not characterized by chronic sputum production.
• Option D: Pulmonary hydatidosis is a parasitic infection caused by the tapeworm Echinococcus granulosus. It can lead to the formation of cysts in various organs, including the lungs. While pulmonary hydatidosis can cause respiratory symptoms, it is less likely to be associated with a persistent mucopurulent cough as described in the question. Symptoms may include cough, chest pain, and shortness of breath, but the presence of cysts and the clinical picture would typically differ from bronchiectasis.

stem: A 40-year-old male with progressive forward bending with increasing stiffness of the spine. He has increased stiffness in his lower back, hip pain and difficulty breathing. A CT spine was performed and shown below. What is the Most Likely Diagnosis?
subject: orthopaedics, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• Ankylosing Spondylitis is a chronic inflammatory condition that primarily affects the spine and sacroiliac joints. In this case, the patient is experiencing progressive forward bending (known as kyphosis) and increasing stiffness of the spine, which are classic symptoms of Ankylosing Spondylitis. The condition typically starts in the lower back and can cause severe stiffness and pain. It can also lead to fusion of the vertebrae, causing a rigid spine.
• The hallmark of this condition is inflammation and eventual fusion of the vertebrae, leading to a rigid spine. The CT spine image is often used to visualize the extent of spinal involvement in Ankylosing Spondylitis, which may show characteristic features such as bamboo spine or syndesmophytes (bony growths between the vertebrae).

Other Options:
• Option A: Rheumatoid Arthritis is an autoimmune condition that primarily affects the joints, especially the small joints of the hands and feet. It is characterized by joint pain, swelling, and stiffness. Unlike Ankylosing Spondylitis, Rheumatoid Arthritis does not typically involve the spine to the same extent. It can cause deformities in the fingers and other joints but is not known for causing the progressive forward bending and stiffness of the spine seen in this patient.
• Option C: Reiter's syndrome, also known as reactive arthritis, is a type of inflammatory arthritis that can develop in response to infections in other parts of the body, such as the urinary tract or gastrointestinal tract. While joint pain is a common symptom of Reiter's syndrome, it is usually associated with other symptoms like eye inflammation (conjunctivitis) and urinary symptoms (urethritis). The hallmark of Ankylosing Spondylitis is primarily related to spinal involvement and stiffness, making Reiter's syndrome less likely in this context.
• Option D: Fluorosis is a condition caused by excessive fluoride intake, often through contaminated drinking water. It primarily affects the bones and teeth. In skeletal fluorosis, the bones may become denser and less flexible, leading to pain and limited mobility. However, it typically affects the long bones, not the spine. The patient's symptoms of progressive forward bending and stiffness in the spine are not characteristic of fluorosis.

stem: A 24-year-old patient comes to the office to evaluate sneezing, cough, fever, and pain behind the eyes. He is being diagnosed with sinusitis. The radiograph shown below is done to better assess the frontal sinus. What is the common name of this view?
subject: ENT, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• Caldwell's view is a radiographic projection used to assess the frontal sinus. In this view, the X-ray beam is directed through the patient's forehead, and the film or detector is placed behind the patient's head. This positioning allows for clear visualization of the frontal sinus, making it a valuable tool for diagnosing conditions like sinusitis involving the frontal sinuses.

Other Options:
• Option B: The Towne view is a radiographic projection used to visualize the occipital bone and the foramen magnum at the base of the skull. It is primarily used to assess structures in the posterior cranial fossa and is not specific to assessing the frontal sinus. This view is valuable for diagnosing conditions related to the base of the skull but is unrelated to frontal sinus evaluation.
• Option C: The Waters view is a radiographic projection used to visualize the maxillary sinuses and the orbits. In this view, the patient's chin is elevated, and the X-ray beam is directed upward from below the chin. This positioning allows for clear visualization of the maxillary sinuses and orbits, making it useful for assessing these structures. However, it is not designed for assessing the frontal sinus, which is the focus of the question.
• Option D: Pierre's view includes the Waters view with the patient's mouth open, it can allow for visualization of the sphenoid sinus in addition to the maxillary sinuses and orbits. This view provides a broader assessment of sinus structures compared to the Waters view alone. However, it is still distinct from Caldwell's view, which specifically targets the frontal sinus.

stem: A patient, age 54, came to OPD with extreme pain in all body joints, fever and a history of weight loss. He is diagnosed with metastatic bone cancer. Which of the following test will be done to confirm the bone cancer?
subject: orthopaedics, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• Bone scans are commonly used to detect and confirm the presence of metastatic bone cancer. In this imaging test, a small amount of radioactive material is injected into the patient's bloodstream.
• This material tends to accumulate in areas of the bone where there may be abnormal growth or metastatic cancer cells.
• Special cameras then capture images of the bones, highlighting any areas of increased radioactivity. These areas of increased activity are indicative of bone abnormalities, including metastatic cancer lesions.

Other Options:
• Option A: A Quantitative CT scan can provide detailed images of the bones, but it is more commonly used for assessing bone density and structure rather than confirming metastatic bone cancer. It may be useful for evaluating the extent of bone involvement once bone cancer is suspected.
• Option C: X-rays are helpful for visualizing bones and can show abnormalities in bone structure, but they may not be as sensitive as bone scans in detecting early or small metastatic lesions.
• Option D: Dexa scan (Dual-Energy X-ray Absorptiometry) is primarily used to measure bone density and is commonly used in the diagnosis of osteoporosis and other bone-related conditions. It is not designed for detecting metastatic bone cancer.

stem: A 22-year-old male is brought to the emergency department by his father 3-hours after a fall from the roof. The patient complains of abdominal pain only. The patient is alert and oriented to time and space. He is not confused. The patient has a blood pressure of 90/60 mmHg. The other physical examination of the patient is unremarkable except for a mild swelling in the umbilical region. The doctor advised a FAST scan. Which of the following area is evaluated along with the abdomen during the investigation involved in the patient's pathology?
subject: Surgery, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• In this scenario, the doctor has advised a FAST scan, which stands for "Focused Assessment with Sonography for Trauma." When performing a FAST scan in a trauma patient, the thorax (Option A) is one of the areas evaluated along with the abdomen.
• A FAST scan is a rapid bedside ultrasound examination used to assess trauma patients for the presence of fluid in the abdominal and thoracic cavities. It is a valuable tool for quickly identifying potential life-threatening injuries, such as internal bleeding.
• While the primary focus of the FAST scan is on the abdomen, it can also evaluate the thorax for the presence of blood or fluid in the pleural cavity. In this case, the patient had a fall from the roof and complained of abdominal pain, so the doctor is performing a FAST scan not only to assess the abdomen but also to check for any signs of thoracic trauma or bleeding in the chest cavity.

Other Options:
• Option B: In a FAST scan, the primary focus is on the abdomen and thorax, and while the pelvis may be assessed during a trauma evaluation, it is not typically part of the FAST scan itself. Pelvic trauma may require separate imaging techniques, such as a pelvic X-ray or CT scan.
• Option C: A FAST scan does not specifically assess peripheral blood vessels. It is primarily focused on identifying the presence of fluid or bleeding in the abdominal and thoracic cavities.
• Option D: A FAST scan is not used to evaluate the cranial cavity. In cases of head trauma or suspected intracranial injuries, different imaging modalities like CT scans or MRI scans of the head are typically employed.
